concepts.description: Cells were first discovered by Robert Hooke in 1665 using a primitive microscope. He observed cells in a cork slice and called them 'cells' because they resembled a honeycomb.
Unicellular organisms are made up of a single cell, while multicellular organisms are composed of many cells that work together to form various body parts.
A cell has three main features: plasma membrane, nucleus, and cytoplasm. The plasma membrane is a selectively permeable membrane that separates the cell from its external environment.

The plasma membrane is a selectively permeable membrane that allows certain substances to pass through while keeping others out. It plays a crucial role in maintaining the cell's internal environment.

Diffusion is the process by which substances move from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration. It plays a crucial role in the exchange of gases and substances between the cell and its environment.

Osmosis is the movement of water molecules across a selectively permeable membrane from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration. It helps regulate the balance of fluids within the cell.

A hypotonic solution has a lower concentration of solutes than the cell, causing the cell to swell. An isotonic solution has the same concentration of solutes as the cell, causing no net movement of water. A hypertonic solution has a higher concentration of solutes than the cell, causing the cell to shrink.

concepts.concept_title: Division of Labour in Multicellular Organisms
concepts.description: In multicellular organisms, cells specialize in specific functions and work together to achieve efficiency. This specialization leads to the formation of tissues, which are groups of cells that work together to perform a particular function.

Meristematic tissue is a type of tissue that consists of actively dividing cells, which are responsible for the growth of plants. It is located at specific regions, such as the tips of roots and stems, and is classified into apical, lateral, and intercalary meristems.

Permanent tissue is a type of tissue that is formed when meristematic cells differentiate and lose the ability to divide. It can be simple or complex, and its cells can be living or dead. Examples of permanent tissues include parenchyma, collenchyma, and sclerenchyma.

Parenchyma is a type of simple permanent tissue that consists of relatively unspecialized cells with thin cell walls. It is found in various parts of plants, such as the stem, root, and leaves, and can store food, water, and other substances.

Collenchyma is a type of simple permanent tissue that consists of living cells with thickened cell walls. It provides mechanical support and flexibility to plants, allowing them to bend without breaking.
Sclerenchyma is a type of simple permanent tissue that consists of dead cells with thick, lignified cell walls. It provides mechanical support and protection to plants, making them stiff and hard.

Epidermis is the outermost layer of cells in plants, providing a protective barrier against water loss, mechanical injury, and invasion by pathogens. It can be a single layer of cells or multiple layers, depending on the plant species.
Stomata are small pores found in the epidermis of leaves, responsible for gas exchange and transpiration. They are surrounded by guard cells, which regulate the opening and closing of stomata.

Vascular tissue is a type of complex tissue that consists of xylem and phloem. Xylem is responsible for transporting water and minerals from the roots to the leaves, while phloem transports food and other organic compounds from the leaves to the rest of the plant.
Xylem is a type of vascular tissue that consists of tracheids, vessels, xylem parenchyma, and xylem fibers. It is responsible for transporting water and minerals from the roots to the leaves.

Phloem is a type of vascular tissue that consists of sieve cells, sieve tubes, companion cells, phloem fibers, and phloem parenchyma. It is responsible for transporting food and other organic compounds from the leaves to the rest of the plant.

Food security depends on availability and access to food, and it is essential for a country's development. Sustainable practices in agriculture and animal husbandry are necessary to ensure food security.

Crop yields can be improved through various methods such as crop variety improvement, crop production improvement, and crop protection management. These methods help increase food production and ensure food security.

Crop variety improvement involves developing new crop varieties with desirable traits such as disease resistance, high yields, and improved quality. This can be achieved through hybridization and genetic modification.

Desirable agronomic characteristics such as higher yield, improved quality, and resistance to biotic and abiotic stresses are essential for crop improvement. These characteristics help increase crop productivity and ensure food security.
key_terms: resistance to biotic and abiotic stresses

concepts.description: Crop production management involves the use of various practices such as nutrient management, irrigation, and pest management to increase crop yields. These practices help optimize crop growth and reduce losses.
Nutrient management involves the use of fertilizers and manure to provide essential nutrients to crops. This helps optimize crop growth and increase yields.

Macronutrients such as nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium are essential for plant growth, while micronutrients such as iron and zinc are required in smaller quantities. Deficiencies in these nutrients can affect plant growth and productivity.

Fertilizers and manure are used to provide essential nutrients to crops. Fertilizers are commercially produced, while manure is prepared from animal excreta and plant waste. Both help optimize crop growth and increase yields.

The characteristics that distinguish living organisms from non-living things, including molecular movement and organization. Living organisms exhibit growth, reproduction, and response to stimuli, and they maintain homeostasis and undergo evolution.

The processes that occur in living organisms to maintain their structure and function, including nutrition, respiration, transportation, and excretion. These processes are necessary for the organization and maintenance of life.

The process by which autotrophs, such as plants and some bacteria, produce their own food through photosynthesis, using energy from sunlight, carbon dioxide, and water. This process is essential for the survival of autotrophs and indirectly supports heterotrophs.

The process by which heterotrophs, such as animals and fungi, obtain their energy and nutrients by consuming other organisms or organic matter. This process involves the breakdown of complex molecules into simpler ones that can be used by the heterotroph.

The process by which organisms transport essential substances, such as nutrients and oxygen, from one part of the body to another. This process is necessary for the maintenance of life and can occur through various mechanisms, including diffusion and circulation.

The process by which organisms remove waste products from their bodies, which is essential for maintaining homeostasis and overall health. Excretion can occur through various mechanisms, including diffusion, filtration, and excretory systems.

The process by which autotrophs, such as plants and some bacteria, produce their own food using energy from sunlight, carbon dioxide, and water. Photosynthesis is essential for life on Earth and occurs in specialized organelles called chloroplasts.

The process by which organisms break down glucose and other organic molecules to produce energy in the form of ATP. Respiration can occur aerobically, using oxygen, or anaerobically, without oxygen, and is essential for the maintenance of life.

The process by which plants absorb essential nutrients, such as water and minerals, from the soil through their roots. Nutrient uptake is necessary for plant growth and development and can occur through various mechanisms, including diffusion and active transport.

The structure and function of stomata, which are small openings on the surface of plant leaves that allow for gas exchange, including carbon dioxide uptake and oxygen release. Stomata are essential for photosynthesis and can be regulated to conserve water.

concepts.concept_title: Control and Coordination in Living Organisms
concepts.description: Control and coordination are essential for visible movements in response to environmental changes, which involves the recognition of events and responses to them.

concepts.concept_title: Types of Movement in Living Organisms
concepts.description: Movements can be growth-related, as in plants, or growth-independent, as in animals and some plants.
Movements in living organisms are often associated with life due to their response to environmental changes, which is a key characteristic of living beings.
Control and coordination are necessary for making appropriate movements in response to environmental changes.

concepts.concept_title: Role of Specialised Tissues in Control and Coordination
concepts.description: Specialised tissues, like nervous and muscular tissues, are used to provide control and coordination in multicellular organisms.
Environmental changes are detected by receptors in sense organs, such as the inner ear, the nose, the tongue, and others.
Nervous impulses travel from the dendrite to the cell body, and then along the axon to its end, setting off the release of chemicals in the process.

concepts.concept_title: Role of Nervous Tissue in Conducting Information
concepts.description: Nervous tissue is made up of an organised network of neurons and is specialised for conducting information via electrical impulses from one part of the body to another.
A neuron consists of a dendrite, cell body, and axon, and is capable of detecting and transmitting nervous impulses.
Specialized structures that receive and respond to stimuli from the environment, responsible for converting physical stimuli into electrical signals.
The process of converting stimuli into electrical signals for onward transmission, involving nerve impulses as a medium. The role of dendrites, axons, and synapses in information transmission.

concepts.concept_title: Control and Coordination through Reflex Arcs
concepts.description: A rapid response mechanism to environmental changes, involving a simple neural pathway in the spinal cord for quick decision-making and action.

concepts.concept_title: Role of Brain in Thinking Process
concepts.description: The brain's function in complex thinking, involving the creation of nerve impulses from many neurons and the integration of information from various parts of the body.
The evolutionary advantages of reflex arcs in animals without complex thought processes, serving as efficient ways of functioning in response to rapid changes in the environment.
A reflex arc is a sequence of events, including the reception of stimulus, transmission of signal to the spinal cord, and response of the effector organ.
The CNS includes the brain and spinal cord, responsible for receiving and integrating information from the body.
The PNS consists of cranial nerves and spinal nerves, facilitating communication between the CNS and other body parts.
The fore-brain is responsible for thinking, interpreting sensory information, and controlling voluntary movements.
The mid-brain and hind-brain control involuntary actions, including blood pressure, salivation, and vomiting.
The cerebellum is responsible for precision of voluntary actions, maintaining posture, and balance.
The brain is protected by a bony box and a fluid-filled balloon which provides shock absorption, and the spinal cord is protected by the vertebral column.
Muscle cells change their shape and shorten in response to nervous electrical impulses, due to the rearrangement of special proteins.

concepts.concept_title: Difference between Voluntary and Involuntary Muscles
concepts.description: Voluntary muscles are controlled by the nervous system and are responsible for movements like walking and walking, whereas involuntary muscles act without conscious control and maintain body functions like digestion and respiration.
A reflex action is a rapid, automatic response to a stimulus, whereas walking is a voluntary movement of the body.
A synapse is the region where two neurons communicate with each other through electrical and chemical signals.

concepts.concept_title: Role of the Brain in Reflex Action
concepts.description: The brain plays a crucial role in reflex action by processing the information received from the sensory receptors and sending out the necessary signals to the muscles.
Plants respond to stimuli through electrical-chemical means, without the presence of nervous tissue or muscle tissue, and this information is conveyed from cell to cell.

Plants exhibit two types of movement: one dependent on growth (like the directional movement of a seedling) and the other independent of growth (like the immediate response of the sensitive plant to touch).
Plant cells change shape by altering the amount of water, leading to swelling, shrinking, and changing shapes.
Plants exhibit directional growth or movement in response to stimuli such as light or gravity.
Plants exhibit different types of tropic movements, including phototropism, geotropism, hydrotropism, and chemotropism.

concepts.concept_title: Communication of information through electrical impulses in plants
concepts.description: Electrical impulses are a means of rapid information transfer in plant bodies.

concepts.concept_title: Limitations of electrical impulses for communication
concepts.description: Electrical impulses have limitations, such as only affecting connected cells and requiring cell reset time.
Cells use chemical communication to convey information between each other, as electrical impulse generation is limited.
Plant hormones are chemical messengers that regulate plant growth, development, and responses to the environment.

concepts.concept_title: Auxin and Its Role in Plant Growth
concepts.description: Auxin promotes plant growth by stimulating cell elongation, particularly in the shoot tip and on the shady side of the stem.
Gibberellins promote the growth of the stem, similar to auxins.
Cytokinins promote cell division in areas of rapid growth, such as fruits and seeds.

concepts.concept_title: Abscisic Acid and Inhibition of Plant Growth
concepts.description: Abscisic acid inhibits plant growth by promoting leaf wilting.

concepts.concept_title: Hormonal Means of Information Transmission in Animals
concepts.description: Animals use hormonal signals for information transmission, such as adrenaline for stress responses.
A system that constitutes a second way of control and coordination in the body through hormone secretion.
Hormones that perform various functions, including regulated growth and development in the body.
A hormone secreted by the pituitary gland that regulates growth and development of the body.
A mechanism through which hormones regulate growth and development in the body.
Mechanisms through which hormones are secreted in precise quantities, and the timing and amount of hormone released are regulated.
A hormone produced by the pancreas that helps in regulating blood sugar levels.
A hormone produced by the thyroid gland that regulates carbohydrate, protein, and fat metabolism in the body.
Glands that secrete hormones to regulate various bodily functions.
A gland that secretes hormones to regulate growth and development of the body, including the release of growth hormone.
Hormones are secreted by endocrine glands with specific functions, controlling various physiological processes.
List of hormones and their corresponding endocrine glands or functions, including growth hormone, thyroxin, insulin, testosterone, adrenaline, and releasing hormone.
Chemical coordination is seen in animals where hormones produced in one part of the body move to another part to achieve the desired effect.
A feedback mechanism regulates the action of hormones in the body to maintain homeostasis.
Example of a plant hormone is cytokinin.
The gap between two neurons is called a synapse.
The brain is responsible for thinking, regulating heartbeat, and balancing the body.
Receptors receive signals and respond to stimuli, maintaining homeostasis in the body.
A neuron receives signals, processes them, and transmits signals to other neurons or muscles.
Chemical coordination in plants is seen through mechanisms such as phototropism.
A system of control and coordination is necessary for maintaining homeostasis and integrating various physiological processes in an organism.
Involuntary actions are those that occur without conscious control, while reflex actions are automatic responses to stimuli.
Both nervous and hormonal mechanisms are involved in control and coordination in animals, working together to achieve desired responses.

concepts.concept_title: Movement mechanisms in plants and animals
concepts.description: Mechanisms of movement differ between plants and animals, with plants exhibiting tropisms and animals using voluntary and involuntary actions.
Organisms reproduce to create more individuals, which is essential for species to exist and be noticeable. This process helps in the creation of new individuals that resemble their parents.

concepts.concept_title: Similarity among organisms due to reproduction
concepts.description: Reproducing organisms create new individuals that look very much like themselves due to similarities in their body designs.

concepts.concept_title: DNA as the blueprint for body design
concepts.description: DNA (Deoxyribo Nucleic Acid) molecules in the cell nucleus contain the information for inheritance of features from parents to the next generation.

concepts.concept_title: Creation of DNA copies through chemical reactions
concepts.description: Cells use chemical reactions to build copies of their DNA, resulting in two copies of the DNA in a reproducing cell.
DNA copies separate from each other, each with its own cellular apparatus, during cell division.
The process of copying DNA may have some variations, leading to similar but not identical DNA copies in the new cells.
The tendency for variation during reproduction is the basis for evolution, allowing populations of organisms to adapt to their niches in the ecosystem.

concepts.concept_title: Importance of DNA Copying in Reproduction
concepts.description: DNA copying is crucial for reproduction as it ensures the transfer of genetic information from parents to offspring.

concepts.concept_title: Beneficial Role of Variation in Species Survival
concepts.description: Variation within a population allows individuals to adapt to changing environmental conditions, contributing to the survival of species over time.
Binary fission is a type of asexual reproduction where a unicellular organism divides into two equal cells, resulting in the formation of two new individuals.

concepts.concept_title: Types of Fission in Unicellular Organisms
concepts.description: Unicellular organisms exhibit different patterns of fission, including simple splitting, Amoeba-like fission, and multiple fission, as seen in organisms such as Leishmania and Plasmodium.

Multiple fission is a type of asexual reproduction where a unicellular organism divides into multiple cells simultaneously, resulting in the formation of many new individuals, as seen in organisms such as Plasmodium.
Fragmentation is a type of asexual reproduction where an organism breaks into smaller fragments, each of which develops into a new individual, as seen in organisms such as starfish and flatworms.

concepts.concept_title: Different types of reproduction in multicellular organisms
concepts.description: Multicellular organisms use various methods for reproduction, including cell division, budding, regeneration, and vegetative propagation.

concepts.concept_title: Role of specialised cells in reproduction
concepts.description: Reproduction in multicellular organisms is often carried out by a specific type of cell, while other cell types perform different functions.
Regeneration is the ability of an organism to give rise to a new individual from a body part, resulting from the organised sequence of cell changes.

concepts.concept_title: Process of budding in Hydra and Planaria
concepts.description: Budding is a reproductive process in Hydra and Planaria, where regenerative cells produce an outgrowth that develops into a new individual.
Plants can reproduce vegetatively, producing new plants from parts like roots, stems, and leaves, under appropriate conditions.

concepts.concept_title: Comparative analysis of methods of reproduction
concepts.description: This concept involves the comparison and understanding of different methods of reproduction used in multicellular organisms, including cell division, budding, regeneration, and vegetative propagation.

concepts.concept_title: Adaptation and advantages of vegetative propagation
concepts.description: Vegetative propagation allows for faster reproduction, early flowering and fruiting, and can be used for the propagation of plants that have lost seed production capacity.
Regeneration occurs in some plants when a piece is cut and placed in a moist environment, resulting in the growth of new shoots and roots.
Binary fission involves the splitting of a cell into two equal parts, while multiple fission involves the splitting of a cell into more than two parts.
Vegetative propagation involves growing new individuals from plant parts such as leaves, stems, or roots, without seeds.
Tissue culture involves growing new plants in a controlled environment from plant tissues or cells.
Spore formation involves the production of spores, which can develop into new individuals, in some organisms such as Rhizopus.
Asexual reproduction involves the creation of new individuals from a single parent, without the involvement of another individual.
Sexual mode of reproduction involves the creation of new individuals from the combination of genetic material from two parents.

concepts.concept_title: Importance of Sexual Mode of Reproduction
concepts.description: Sexual mode of reproduction allows for genetic variation and ensures the survival of species by providing a pool of variations.
DNA-copying mechanisms are not absolutely accurate and produce variations at a slow pace. Accumulated variations from previous generations create new combinations of variants.
Each new generation would end up having twice the amount of DNA if DNA was not reduced before reproduction. Meiosis solves this problem by halving the number of chromosomes and DNA.
In complex organisms, germ-cells specialize to form different types of gametes, one with stored food and the other motile. This leads to differences in male and female reproductive organs and bodies.

concepts.concept_title: Functions of Female and Male Gametes
concepts.description: Female gametes have stored food and are larger, while male gametes are motile and smaller.
Flowers can be unisexual or bisexual and contain either or both stamens and pistils.
Stamen is the male reproductive part and produces yellowish pollen grains.
Pistil is the female reproductive part with three parts: ovary, middle elongated part, and a swollen bottom part.
Pollen grains are produced by the stamen, which is the male reproductive part in flowers.

concepts.concept_title: Stigmatic Surface and Pollen Tube Formation
concepts.description: Pollen tubes form on the stigmatic surface, allowing the male germ cell to fuse with the female gamete in the ovule.

Self-pollination occurs when pollen is transferred from the stamen to the stigma in the same flower, while cross-pollination involves transferring pollen from one flower to another through agents like wind, water or animals.
Fertilisation occurs when the male germ cell fuses with the female gamete, resulting in the formation of a zygote capable of growing into a new plant.
Seed formation occurs after fertilisation, where the ovule develops into a seed containing the future plant embryo, which germinates under appropriate conditions.
Humans use a sexual mode of reproduction, involving changes in body growth, development of secondary sexual characteristics, and formation of gametes (sperm and egg cells) through meiosis.
Puberty is characterised by physical and hormonal changes in the body, leading to the development of secondary sexual characteristics, such as breast growth in girls and facial hair growth in boys.
Menstruation occurs when the female reproductive system prepares for pregnancy, and is triggered by hormonal changes during puberty.

concepts.concept_title: Development of Male and Female Gametes
concepts.description: Gametes (sperm and egg cells) develop through meiosis, a type of cell division that results in cells with half the number of chromosomes.
Sexual maturity is a gradual process that occurs over months and years, influenced by hormonal changes and the need for reproduction.
Hormones play a crucial role in regulating puberty, including the secretion of testosterone, which influences sperm formation and changes in appearance during puberty.
The male reproductive system consists of testes, vas deferens, and accessory glands like the prostate and seminal vesicles, which produce and transport sperms.
Sperms are tiny bodies consisting mainly of genetic material and a long tail that helps them move, produced through spermatogenesis in the testes.
Sexual maturity is essential for reproduction, involving changes in appearance, growth of hair, and development of reproductive organs.
Ovaries produce eggs and hormones, while oviducts carry eggs to the uterus. The uterus is an elastic bag-like structure connected to the vagina.
The uterus prepares for fertilization every month by thickening its lining. If fertilization doesn't occur, the lining breaks down and is shed as menstruation.
Fertilization occurs in the oviduct when a sperm meets an egg, forming a zygote. The zygote implants in the uterine lining, where it develops into a fetus.

concepts.concept_title: Role of Placenta in Nutrition and Waste Removal
concepts.description: The placenta provides a large surface area for glucose and oxygen to pass from the mother to the embryo, and also removes waste substances from the embryo.
Reproductive health involves considering the consequences of sexual activity, including the potential for sexually transmitted diseases and the ability to care for a child.
Condoms or coverings worn on the penis or vagina prevent sperm from reaching the egg, thereby preventing pregnancy.
Drugs that alter the hormonal balance of the body to prevent egg release and fertilization, but can cause side effects.
Devices like copper-T or loops inserted in the uterus to prevent pregnancy, but can cause side effects due to irritation.
Surgical methods to block the vas deferens in males or fallopian tubes in females to prevent fertilization.
Reproduction process increases population size, which can affect standard of living, but inequality in society is a more significant factor.
Reproduction involves DNA copying and creation of a new individual through sexual or asexual means, with variations created by DNA copying mechanisms.
Certain organisms like bacteria, protozoa, and hydra reproduce through fission or regeneration, or undergo vegetative propagation.
A mode of reproduction where new generations are created from a single individual, such as in plants and some animals.
A process involving two individuals, DNA copying, and creation of a new individual, resulting in variations.
Modes of sexual reproduction allow for greater variation to be generated.
The transfer of pollen grains from anther to stigma is referred to as pollination.
Fertilisation occurs in the fallopian tube.
The male reproductive system consists of testes, vas deferens, seminal vesicles, prostate gland, urethra, and penis.
The female reproductive system consists of ovaries, fallopian tubes, uterus, and vagina.
Sexual reproduction in human beings involves the introduction of sperm in the vagina of the female.
Contraception can be achieved by the use of condoms, oral pills, copper-T, and other methods to avoid pregnancy.

concepts.concept_title: Difference between Asexual and Sexual Reproduction
concepts.description: Asexual reproduction takes place through budding, etc., while sexual reproduction involves the fusion of gametes.
The advantages of sexual reproduction include the production of offspring with increased variation.
The testes produce sperms.
Contraceptive methods can be adopted to avoid pregnancy.
Methods of contraception include the use of condoms, oral pills, copper-T, and other methods.

concepts.concept_title: Difference in Modes of Reproduction in Unicellular and Multicellular Organisms
concepts.description: The modes of reproduction differ in unicellular and multicellular organisms.
Reproduction helps in providing stability to populations of species.

concepts.concept_title: Mechanism of Variation Generation and Inheritance
concepts.description: Variations are created by sexual reproduction and inherited from one generation to the other.

concepts.concept_title: Types of Inheritance and Generation of Offspring
concepts.description: Inheritance from one generation to the other provides both a common basic body design and subtle changes in it.

concepts.concept_title: Creation of Diversity through Sexual Reproduction
concepts.description: Sexual reproduction generates greater diversity than asexual reproduction by shuffling genes.
Environmental factors select survival of variants with advantageous traits, promoting evolutionary processes.
Rules of heredity determine the process by which traits and characteristics are reliably inherited.
Children inherit traits from parents, but also exhibit variations, making each child unique.
Earlobe types can be inherited, and their pattern can be correlated with parental earlobes.
Each trait in a human being is influenced by both paternal and maternal DNA, resulting in two versions of the trait in each child.
Mendel worked out the main rules of inheritance by counting individuals exhibiting a particular trait in each generation, leading to the laws of inheritance.

Traits like 'T' are called dominant traits, while those that behave like 't' are called recessive traits; a single copy of 'T' is enough to make a plant tall, while both copies have to be 't' for the plant to be short.
Mendelian experiments test the expression of traits in F1 and F2 generations by getting both the parental plants and these F1 tall plants to reproduce by self-pollination.

concepts.concept_title: Inheritance of Traits over Two Generations
concepts.description: Traits like 'T' and 't' can be combined in different ways in F1 and F2 generations, leading to a 1:2:1 ratio of TT, Tt, and tt trait combinations.

concepts.concept_title: Tallness and seed shape traits in F1 progeny
concepts.description: In F1 progeny, both dominant traits of tallness and round seeds appear as a combination of two genes. This indicates that these traits are dominant.

concepts.concept_title: Formation of new traits in F2 progeny
concepts.description: In F2 progeny, new combinations of traits appear due to recombination of genes controlling seed shape and seed colour, leading to the independent inheritance of these traits.
Traits can be independently inherited if there is no linkage between the genes controlling these traits, allowing them to be separated in offspring.

concepts.concept_title: Function of genes in controlling traits
concepts.description: Genes control traits by providing instructions for the production of proteins, which in turn regulate characteristics such as plant height.

concepts.concept_title: Role of enzymes in gene function
concepts.description: Enzymes are crucial in gene function, as alterations in enzyme efficiency can affect the expression of traits.
During sexual reproduction, both parents contribute equally to the DNA of the progeny, resulting in the combination of traits.
Germ cells have only one set of genes, which they inherit from the parents, allowing for the independent inheritance of traits.
Chromosomes are composed of separate, independent pieces of DNA, each containing a set of genes, which are inherited independently of each other.
In human beings, the sex of the child is determined by the paternal chromosome being X for girls or Y for boys.
In human beings, women have a pair of sex chromosomes (XX) and men have a mismatched pair of sex chromosomes (XY).

concepts.concept_title: Inheritance of X and Y Chromosomes
concepts.description: The sex of the children will be determined by what they inherit from their father, i.e., an X chromosome for girls and a Y chromosome for boys.
Traits may be dominant or recessive, and the trait that gets expressed is the dominant trait.
Traits in one individual may be inherited separately, giving rise to new combinations of traits in the offspring of sexual reproduction.
Variations arising during the process of reproduction can be inherited and may lead to increased survival of the individuals.

concepts.concept_title: Equal Genetic Contribution of Male and Female Parents
concepts.description: The equal genetic contribution of male and female parents is ensured in the progeny, as both parents contribute one set of genes to the offspring.
Environment refers to the interaction between living and non-living components of the ecosystem.

concepts.concept_title: Biotic and Abiotic Components of Ecosystem
concepts.description: Biotic components include living organisms, while abiotic components include physical factors like temperature, rainfall, wind, and soil.
Natural ecosystems include forests, ponds, and lakes, while human-made ecosystems include gardens, crop-fields, and aquariums.

concepts.concept_title: Designing an Aquarium as a Human-Made Ecosystem
concepts.description: An aquarium requires a free space for swimming, water, oxygen, and food, and can be maintained as a self-sustaining system with aquatic plants and animals.

concepts.concept_title: Importance of Cleaning an Aquarium or Pond
concepts.description: Aquariums and ponds need to be cleaned regularly to maintain a healthy ecosystem, and decomposers play a crucial role in breaking down dead organisms.

concepts.concept_title: Classification of Organisms as Producers, Consumers, and Decomposers
concepts.description: Producers make organic compounds like sugar and starch from inorganic substances using sunlight and chlorophyll, while consumers depend on producers for their sustenance, and decomposers break down complex organic substances.

concepts.concept_title: Classification of Consumers as Herbivores, Carnivores, Omnivores, and Parasites
concepts.description: Herbivores consume only plants, carnivores consume only animals, omnivores consume both plants and animals, and parasites depend on a host organism for their sustenance.

concepts.concept_title: Role of Decomposers in Breaking Down Dead Organisms
concepts.description: Decomposers like bacteria and fungi break down dead organisms and waste products into simple inorganic substances, which are used by plants.
Autotrophs fix up solar energy and make it available for heterotrophs. They form the first trophic level.
Herbivores or primary consumers come at the second trophic level, feeding on autotrophs.
When green plants are eaten by primary consumers, a great deal of energy is lost as heat to the environment.
An average of 10% of the food eaten is turned into its own body and made available for the next level of consumers.

concepts.concept_title: Food Chains and Number of Steps
concepts.description: Trophic levels food chains generally consist of only three or four steps due to significant energy loss.
The relationship between organisms can be shown as a series of branching lines called a food web.
The flow of energy is unidirectional from autotrophs to herbivores and decomposers.
The availability of energy decreases as we move up the trophic levels.
The depletion of the ozone layer is caused by chemicals responsible for the breakup of ozone molecules. These chemicals are released through human activities.
Biodegradable substances are broken down by living organisms, while non-biodegradable substances persist for a long time in the environment.

concepts.concept_title: Effects of Biodegradable Substances on the Environment
concepts.description: Biodegradable substances may release nutrients or create ideal breeding conditions for microorganisms, affecting the environment.

concepts.concept_title: Effects of Non-biodegradable Substances on the Environment
concepts.description: Non-biodegradable substances persist in the environment, potentially causing harm to organisms and disrupting ecosystems.
Cities and towns have garbage disposal systems in place to manage waste, including collecting, treating, and disposing of waste.
Disposal of waste has serious environmental implications, and it's essential to consider biodegradable and non-biodegradable waste separately.

concepts.concept_title: Impact of Human Activities on Environment
concepts.description: Human activities can significantly affect the environment, including waste generation and chemical usage.
The ozone layer protects against ultraviolet radiation from the Sun, and its depletion has serious environmental consequences.

concepts.concept_title: Effects of Waste Disposal on Ecosystems
concepts.description: Disposal of waste can cause environmental problems, including pollution, damage to soil and water bodies.
Trophic levels in a food-chain are limited due to energy loss, and removal of organisms from one trophic level affects the entire ecosystem.
Biological magnification occurs when pollutants become concentrated at higher trophic levels, potentially harming organisms.
Practices like carrying cloth bags, switching off lights, and walking to school can help reduce waste and promote environmental conservation.
Ecosystem components are interdependent, and removal of organisms from one trophic level can have significant effects on the entire ecosystem.
Proper waste disposal and recycling of materials like plastics are essential for reducing environmental pollution.
The levels of magnification can vary depending on the ecosystem, affecting our ability to study and understand its processes.
Non-biodegradable wastes cause environmental issues, including pollution and harm to living organisms.
Biodegradable waste may not have a significant environmental impact, but its effects must be studied carefully.
Damage to the ozone layer is a cause for concern due to its role in protecting life on Earth from harmful UV radiation.
Various measures are being taken to limit damage to the ozone layer and mitigate its effects.
The number and types of organisms present on earth, estimated to be between 1.7-1.8 million.
The process of standardizing the naming of living organisms, using a scientific name accepted worldwide.
The agreed principles and criteria for assigning scientific names to plants.
The established procedures for assigning scientific names to animals.
A system of providing a two-component name (genus and specific epithet) for organisms.
Rules such as biological names being in Latin, handwritten underlining/italics, capitalization, and author abbreviation.
The process of grouping organisms into categories based on easily observable characters.
Taxa are defined as convenient categories we use to study organisms, and can represent categories at different levels.
Living organisms can be classified based on their characteristics into different taxa, and this process is known as taxonomy.
Taxonomy involves characterisation, identification, classification, and nomenclature, and is the basis of modern taxonomic studies.
Systematics is the study of the relationships among different organisms and their diversities, and includes the identification, nomenclature, and classification of organisms.
Taxonomic categories and hierarchy are illustrated by the example of insects, and include ranks like kingdom, phylum, class, order, family, genus, and species.
A taxon, also known as a unit of classification, represents a rank and is a distinct biological entity.
Organisms can be classified into different ranks such as kingdom, phylum, class, order, family, genus, and species.
A species is a group of individual organisms with fundamental similarities. It is identified based on distinct morphological differences.
A genus is a group of related species with more characters in common than species of other genera. It is an aggregate of closely related species.
Multiple species can belong to a single genus based on their morphological similarities. For example, potato and brinjal are different species but both belong to the genus Solanum.
A family is a group of related genera with still fewer similarities compared to genus and species. Families are characterized on the basis of both vegetative and reproductive features.

concepts.concept_title: Family and Its Relationship with Other Categories
concepts.description: Families are composed of related genera, and genera are composed of related species. A family is placed within a higher category based on shared characteristics.
An order is a higher category that is an assemblage of families with similar characteristics. Orders are less specific compared to families and include a wide range of organisms.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship Among Categories: Order, Family, and Genus
concepts.description: Orders are composed of families, and families are composed of genera, which are in turn composed of species. These categories are related based on shared characteristics.
A class is a higher category that includes related orders. Classes are typically defined by a specific set of characteristics unique to a certain group of organisms.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship Between Class and Other Categories
concepts.description: Classes are composed of related orders, and orders are composed of related families, which are in turn composed of related genera and species. These categories are related based on shared characteristics.
A phylum is the highest classification within the animal kingdom. It includes classes with common features such as a notochord and dorsal hollow neural system.
The highest category of classification is the kingdom. In the animal kingdom, all animals are grouped together, while in the plant kingdom, all plants are grouped together.
Biologists have evolved certain rules and principles for identification, nomenclature, and classification of organisms. Taxonomy is the branch of knowledge dealing with these aspects.
Living organisms are classified to facilitate the study of kinds and diversity of organisms.
There are many categories/ranks which are generally referred to as taxonomic categories or taxa. These categories constitute a taxonomic hierarchy.
Each organism is identified and assigned a correct scientific/biological name comprising two words as per the binomial system of nomenclature.
Different criteria may be chosen to classify organisms, depending on the context, such as characteristics, habits, etc.

A taxon is a group of organisms classified together based on the characteristics they share. Examples of taxa at different hierarchical levels include species, genus, family, order, class, phylum, and kingdom.
The correct sequence of taxonomic categories is species, genus, family, order, class, phylum, and kingdom.

concepts.concept_title: Phylum, Class, Family, Order, Genus, and Species
concepts.description: Define and understand the following terms: (i) Phylum, (ii) Class, (iii) Family, (iv) Order, (v) Genus.
Illustrate the taxonomic hierarchy with suitable examples of a plant and an animal.
Aristotle classified plants into trees, shrubs, and herbs and divided animals into two groups based on red blood.
Linnaeus' Two Kingdom system included Plantae and Animalia kingdoms.
The Two Kingdom system did not distinguish between eukaryotes and prokaryotes, unicellular and multicellular organisms.

concepts.concept_title: Relevance of Cell Structure in Classification
concepts.description: Cell structure, body organisation, mode of nutrition, reproduction, and phylogenetic relationships are relevant in biological classification.

concepts.concept_title: Five Kingdom System of R.H. Whittaker
concepts.description: R.H. Whittaker proposed a Five Kingdom system, which includes Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae, and Animalia.

concepts.concept_title: Kingdom Characteristics of Five Kingdom System
concepts.description: Table 2.1 gives a comparative account of different characteristics of the five kingdoms.
Prokaryotic cells lack a cell nucleus, cell wall, and have a non-desirable appearance.
Eukaryotic cells have a cell nucleus and various other complex structures.
Autotrophic organisms produce their own food through photosynthesis.
Heterotrophic organisms cannot produce their own food and obtain it from other organisms.
The three-domain system divides the Kingdom Monera into two domains and leaves the remaining eukaryotic kingdoms in the third domain.

The five kingdom classification system, proposed by R.H. Whittaker, recognizes five main kingdoms: Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae, and Animalia. Each kingdom represents a distinct group of organisms with unique characteristics.
The Kingdom Monera consists of prokaryotic organisms, such as bacteria, which are abundant in various habitats and display diverse metabolic activities.
Bacteria are grouped into four categories based on their shape: Coccus, Bacillus, Vibrio, and Spirillum.
Bacteria exhibit extensive metabolic diversity, including autotrophic and heterotrophic modes of nutrition.
Archaebacteria are a group of bacteria that live in extreme habitats and have distinct cell wall structures.
Methanogens in the gut of ruminant animals produce methane from dung.
Cyanobacteria are photosynthetic autotrophs with chlorophyll a, producing energy from sunlight.
Eubacteria include heterotrophic, photosynthetic, and chemosynthetic autotrophs with diverse roles in the environment.
Some eubacteria cause diseases in humans, crops, and animals, examples include cholera and typhoid.
Bacteria reproduce by fission, producing spores, and have a primitive type of DNA transfer.
Mycoplasma lack a cell wall, are the smallest living cells, and can survive without oxygen.
Protists are single-celled eukaryotes with a well-defined nucleus and membrane-bound organelles.
Chrysophytes include diatoms and golden algae, with photosynthetic and microscopic cells.
Dinoflagellates are marine, photosynthetic organisms with cellulose plates and two flagella.
Red tides are formed by large numbers of certain algae releasing toxins, which can kill marine animals like fish.
Euglenoids are freshwater or marine organisms with a protein-rich layer called pellicle, two flagella, and photosynthetic pigments similar to those in higher plants.
Slime moulds have a saprophytic life cycle with an aggregation called plasmodium that grows and spreads, forming fruiting bodies with resistant spores.
Protozoans are heterotrophic, living as predators or parasites, and are believed to be primitive relatives of animals.
Amoeboid protozoans live in freshwater, seawater, or moist soil, moving and capturing prey using pseudopodia, and may have silica shells.
Flagellated protozoans are free-living or parasitic, have flagella, and can cause diseases like sleeping sickness.
Ciliated protozoans are aquatic organisms with thousands of cilia, a gullet, and coordinated movement to steer food into the gullet.
Sporozoans include diverse organisms with an infectious spore-like stage in their life cycle, such as the malaria parasite Plasmodium.
Fungi are heterotrophic, have a great diversity in morphology and habitat, and are cosmopolitan, occurring in air, water, soil, and on animals and plants.
Fungi reproduce by forming fruiting bodies with resistant spores, with the exception of yeasts which are unicellular.
Most fungi are heterotrophic, absorbing soluble organic matter from dead substrates, with some living as parasites or symbionts.
Fungal reproduction by vegetative means includes fragmentation, fission, and budding, resulting in asexual reproduction.
Asexual reproduction in fungi occurs through spores such as conidia, sporangiospores, and zoospores.
Sexual reproduction in fungi involves fusion of protoplasms, nuclei, and meiosis resulting in haploid spores.
Plasmogamy is the fusion of protoplasms between two motile or non-motile gametes, while karyogamy is the fusion of two nuclei.
Dikaryophase is a stage in fungi where two nuclei per cell occur, resulting in a dikaryotic condition.
Fruiting bodies in fungi are structures where reduction division occurs, leading to the formation of haploid spores.
Phycomycetes are aquatic fungi with aseptate and coenocytic mycelium, reproducing asexually by zoospores or aplanospores.
Ascomycetes are saprophytic fungi with septate mycelium, reproducing asexually by conidia and sexually by ascospores.
Basidiomycetes are fungi with septate mycelium, reproducing asexually by fragmentation and sexually by basidiospores.
Deuteromycetes are fungi with only asexual or vegetative phases known, reproducing by conidia or vegetatively by fragmentation.
Deuteromycetes reproduce only by asexual spores called conidia and have a septate and branched mycelium.
Members of Deuteromycetes are saprophytes, parasites, or decomposers of litter, and help in mineral cycling.
Examples of Deuteromycetes include Alternaria, Colletotrichum, and Trichoderma.
Kingdom Plantae includes eukaryotic chlorophyll-containing organisms with eukaryotic cell structure, prominent chloroplasts, and cell wall mainly made of cellulose.
The life cycle of plants has two distinct phases, diploid sporophytic and haploid gametophytic, that alternate with each other.
Kingdom Animalia includes heterotrophic eukaryotic organisms that are multicellular, lack cell walls, digest food in an internal cavity, and store food reserves as glycogen or fat.
Viruses are non-cellular organisms with an inert crystalline structure, characterized by replication within the host cell.
Viruses can be smaller than bacteria and were first identified by Dmitri Ivanowsky as the causal organism of the mosaic disease of tobacco.
Viruses are composed of genetic material and proteins, with a protein coat called a capsid that protects the nucleic acid.
Viruses can contain either single-stranded RNA, double-stranded RNA, or double-stranded DNA as their genetic material.
Bacteriophages are usually double-stranded DNA viruses that infect bacteria.
The capsid is made up of small subunits called capsomeres, arranged in helical or polyhedral geometric forms.
Viruses can cause various diseases in humans and plants, including mumps, smallpox, herpes, influenza, and viral diseases like AIDS and potato spindle tuber disease.
Viroids are infectious agents smaller than viruses, composed of free RNA and lacking a protein coat.
Prions are infectious agents consisting of abnormally folded protein, causing diseases like mad cow disease and Creutzfeldt-Jacob disease.
Lichens are symbiotic associations between algae and fungi, with algae being autotrophic and fungi being heterotrophic.
Aristotle proposed the first classification system for living organisms into five kingdoms - Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae, and Animalia.
Kingdom Monera includes bacteria, which are cosmopolitan in distribution and show extensive metabolic diversity.
Kingdom Protista includes single-celled eukaryotes like Chrysophytes, Dinoflagellates, Euglenoids, Slime-moulds, and Protozoans.
Kingdom Fungi includes eukaryotic organisms that are saprophytic in their mode of nutrition, with diverse structures and habitats.
Kingdom Plantae includes eukaryotic chlorophyll-containing organisms like algae, bryophytes, pteridophytes, gymnosperms, and angiosperms.
Heterotrophic eukaryotic, multicellular organisms without a cell wall are classified as Kingdom Animalia.
Heterotrophic bacteria and Archaebacteria have distinct economically important uses.
Diatoms have a unique nature of cell-walls.
Algal blooms and red-tides signify specific environmental conditions.
Viroids differ from viruses in their genetic material.
Protozoa are classified into four major groups.
Certain plants exhibit partially heterotrophic characteristics.
Phycobiont and mycobiont signify symbiotic relationships between organisms.
Fungal classes can be compared based on mode of nutrition and mode of reproduction.
Euglenoids exhibit specific characteristic features.
Viruses have a specific structure and nature of genetic material.
Viruses can be classified as living or non-living based on their characteristics.
The Five Kingdom classification system proposed by Whittaker (1969) categorizes living organisms into Monera, Protista, Fungi, Animalia, and Plantae.

Artificial classification systems rely on gross superficial morphological characters, primarily vegetative and androecium structure, which can be affected by environment and separate closely related species.
Natural classification systems consider both external and internal features, like ultra-structure, anatomy, embryology, and phytochemistry, to categorize organisms based on their natural affinities.
Phylogenetic classification systems are based on evolutionary relationships between organisms, assuming that organisms belonging to the same taxa have a common ancestor.
Numerical Taxonomy uses computers to analyze and assign codes to observable characteristics, giving equal importance to each characteristic, and resolving classification difficulties.

Cytotaxonomy is a classification approach based on cytological information like chromosome number, structure, behavior, and chemotaxonomy, which uses chemical constituents of plants to resolve confusions.
Algae are chlorophyll-bearing, autotrophic, and largely aquatic (freshwater and marine) organisms with varying forms and sizes, and reproduce by vegetative, asexual, and sexual methods.
Algae reproduce by producing different types of spores, including zoospores, which are flagellated (motile) and give rise to new plants upon germination.
Algal gametes can be flagellated and similar in size, or non-flagellated, and participate in sexual reproduction through fusion.
Algae can be found in a variety of habitats, including moist stones, soils, and wood, and also associate with fungi (lichens) and animals.
Isogamous reproduction is a type of sexual reproduction where two similar-sized gametes fuse to form a zygote.
Anisogamous reproduction is a type of sexual reproduction where two different-sized gametes fuse to form a zygote.
Oogamous reproduction is a type of sexual reproduction where a large, non-motile female gamete fuses with a smaller, motile male gamete to form a zygote.
Vegetative reproduction in green algae typically occurs through fragmentation.
Asexual reproduction in green algae is typically achieved through the production of flagellated zoospores.
Algae are classified into three main classes: Chlorophyceae (green algae), Phaeophyceae (brown algae), and Rhodophyceae (red algae).
Green algae typically have a rigid cell wall made of cellulose and pectose, with chloroplasts that contain pigments such as chlorophyll a and b.
Brown algae have a cellulosic wall covered with a gelatinous coating of algin, with a centrally located vacuole, nucleus, and plastids.
Brown algae reproduce vegetatively through fragmentation and asexually through the production of biflagellate zoospores.
Pyriform gametes are pear-shaped and bear two laterally attached flagella.

concepts.concept_title: Ectocarpus, Dictyota, Laminaria, Sargassum, and Fucus
concepts.description: These are common examples of oogamous species that exhibit pyriform gametes.
Red algae have a predominance of r-phycoerythrin pigment, store food as floridean starch, and have multicellular bodies.

concepts.concept_title: Methods of reproduction in red algae
concepts.description: Red algae reproduce vegetatively by fragmentation, and asexually and sexually by non-motile spores and gametes respectively.
Rhodophyceae are divided into classes based on pigments, cell wall composition, and habitat.
Sexual reproduction in Rhodophyceae is oogamous and accompanied by complex post-fertilisation developments.
Bryophytes are found in damp, humid, and shaded localities, typically growing in moist areas.

concepts.concept_title: Structure and organisation of bryophyte plant body
concepts.description: Bryophytes have a thallus-like plant body that is prostrate or erect, and attached to the substratum by rhizoids.

concepts.concept_title: Sex organs and gametes in bryophytes
concepts.description: Bryophytes have multicellular sex organs, with the male organ producing biflagellate antherozoids and the female organ producing a single egg.
Bryophytes undergo alternation of generations, with the haploid gametophyte producing spores and diploid sporophyte undergoing meiosis.
Mosses and lichens are the first organisms to colonise rocks and are crucial in decomposing rocks, making them suitable for higher plant growth.

concepts.concept_title: Types of Bryophytes: Liverworts and Mosses
concepts.description: Bryophytes are divided into liverworts and mosses, each having distinct characteristics and reproductive methods.
Liverworts reproduce asexually by fragmentation of thalli or the formation of specialized structures called gemmae, and sexually by the production of male and female sex organs.
The moss life cycle consists of a protonema stage, which develops directly from a spore, and a leafy stage, which develops from the secondary protonema.
Mosses reproduce vegetatively by fragmentation and budding, and sexually by the production of antheridia and archegonia.
Pteridophytes possess vascular tissues - xylem and phloem, and the main plant body is a sporophyte which is differentiated into true root, stem, and leaves.

concepts.concept_title: Structure and Function of Pteridophyte Organs
concepts.description: Pteridophyte organs such as roots, stems, and leaves possess well-differentiated vascular tissues, and the leaves are small (microphylls) or large (macrophylls).
Pteridophytes reproduce by the production of sporangia that produce spores by meiosis in spore mother cells.
A free-living, photosynthetic gametophyte of pteridophytes that requires cool, damp, shady conditions to grow.
Male and female sex organs present on the prothallus, responsible for producing male and female gametes respectively.
Fusion of male and female gametes results in the formation of a zygote, which develops into a multicellular sporophyte.
Homosporous pteridophytes produce similar spores, while heterosporous pteridophytes produce micro and megaspores.
Megaspores germinate into female gametophytes, while microspores develop into male gametophytes.
Ovules are female reproductive structures contained within megasporophylls, which develop into female cones.
A group of plants with naked seeds, in which ovules are not enclosed by any ovary wall.
Gymnosperms produce haploid microspores and megaspores, which develop into male and female gametophytes respectively.
Male and female cones of gymnosperms produce microsporangia and megasporangia respectively, from which microspores and megaspores are produced.
In bryophytes and pteridophytes, the main plant body is the gametophyte which bears male and female sex organs called antheridia and archegonia.
The life cycle of bryophytes includes the formation of gametophyte, antheridia, and archegonia. Zygote formation occurs after fertilisation and eventually produces a sporophyte.
In pteridophytes, the main plant body is the sporophyte which is differentiated into true root, stem, and leaves. Sporangia produce spores which germinate to form gametophytes.
In gymnosperms, the male and female gametophytes do not have an independent free-living existence and remain within the sporangia retained on the sporophytes.
In gymnosperms, the male gametes are carried by pollen grains which grow a pollen tube to reach the archegonia for fertilisation.
In angiosperms, the pollen grains and ovules are developed in specialised structures called flowers, and seeds are enclosed in fruits.
Angiosperms are divided into two classes: dicotyledons and monocotyledons.
The plant kingdom includes algae, bryophytes, pteridophytes, gymnosperms, and angiosperms, which are classified based on their characteristics and reproductive features.
Pollen grain germinates and pollen tube releases the male gamete into the ovule, where it fuses with the egg cell in archegonia.
Zygote develops into embryo and ovules into seeds following fertilisation.
Angiosperms are divided into two classes – dicotyledons and monocotyledons.
Three groups of plants that bear archegonia are liverworts, mosses, and ferns.
Different cell types exhibit various ploidy levels, such as 2n, 2x, and n.

concepts.concept_title: Economic Importance of Algae and Gymnosperms
concepts.description: Algae and gymnosperms have economic importance due to their uses in various sectors.
Heterospory refers to the presence of two types of spores in an organism, which leads to sexual reproduction.
These terms refer to specific structures and processes involved in the life cycle of plants.
Diplontic reproduction refers to a type of asexual reproduction found in plants.

concepts.concept_title: Differentiation between Red Algae and Brown Algae
concepts.description: Red algae and brown algae can be differentiated based on their distinct characteristics.
Gymnosperms exhibit distinct characteristics such as cone-like structures and seeds.
Animals exhibit different levels of organisation of cells, ranging from cellular level to organ system level.

concepts.concept_title: Types of Digestive Systems in Animals
concepts.description: Animals have either incomplete or complete digestive systems.

concepts.concept_title: Types of Circulatory Systems in Animals
concepts.description: Animals have either open or closed circulatory systems.
Animals can be categorised based on their symmetry, being asymmetrical, radially symmetrical, or bilaterally symmetrical.

concepts.concept_title: Diploblastic and Triploblastic Organisation in Animals
concepts.description: Animals can be classified based on their embryonic layers as diploblastic or triploblastic.
Animals can be classified based on the presence or absence of a body cavity called coelom.
Classification of animals is essential for assigning a systematic position to newly described species.

Coelomates are animals possessing a coelom, a fluid-filled body cavity that is lined by mesoderm. Examples of coelomates include annelids, molluscs, arthropods, echinoderms, hemichordates, and chordates.

Pseudocoelomates are animals with a body cavity that is not lined by mesoderm, but instead by scattered pouches of mesoderm between the ectoderm and endoderm. Examples of pseudocoelomates include aschelminthes.
Acoelomates are animals without a body cavity. Examples of acoelomates include platyhelminthes.

Segmentation is the division of the body into repeating segments, with each segment having a similar structure. Metamerism is the phenomenon of segmentation in animals, where each segment has a serial repetition of at least some organs.
Notochord is a rod-like structure formed during embryonic development in some animals, derived from the mesoderm. Animals with notochord are called chordates.
Animals can exhibit radial or bilateral symmetry. Radial symmetry is seen in animals like echinoderms, while bilateral symmetry is seen in animals like humans.
Animal bodies can be organized at different levels, including cellular, tissue, and organ levels. Examples include sponges, cnidarians, and chordates.
Animals can have different types of digestive systems, including intracellular and extracellular digestion. Examples include cnidarians and chordates.
Animals can be classified based on common fundamental features, including body symmetry, body organization, and digestive system. Examples include porifera, coelenterata, and chordata.
Animals have different levels of body organisation, including unicellular, tissue, organ, and organ-system levels.
Platyhelminthes have dorso-ventrally flattened bodies, making them bilaterally symmetrical.
Ctenophores have eight external rows of ciliated comb plates that help in locomotion.
Platyhelminthes have flame cells that help in osmoregulation and excretion.
Aschelminthes, or roundworms, have a complete alimentary canal and a well-developed muscular pharynx.
Aschelminthes are dioecious, with separate males and females.
Annelids are metamerically segmented, with their bodies marked out into distinct segments.
Annelids have paired reproductive organs, including testes and ovaries, which are usually found in males and females.
Arthropods have an organ-system level of organisation and exhibit bilateral symmetry.
The arthropod body is bilaterally symmetrical and consists of head, thorax, and abdomen, covered by a chitinous exoskeleton.
Arthropods have various respiratory organs including gills, book gills, book lungs, or tracheal system.
The circulatory system in arthropods is of open type.
Arthropods have various sensory organs such as antennae, compound and simple eyes, statocysts, and balancing organs.
Excretion in arthropods occurs through malpighian tubules.
Molluscs are bilaterally symmetrical, triploblastic, and coelomate animals with an unsegmented body covered by a calcareous shell.
The molluscan body consists of a distinct head, muscular foot, and visceral hump, along with a soft and spongy mantle.

concepts.concept_title: Respiratory and Excretory Functions in Molluscs
concepts.description: Molluscs have respiratory and excretory functions in the mantle cavity, which houses feather-like gills.
Echinoderms have an endoskeleton of calcareous ossicles and are radially symmetrical as adults, but bilaterally symmetrical as larvae.
The water vascular system in echinoderms helps in locomotion, capture, and transport of food and respiration.
Hemichordates have a rudimentary structure in the collar region called stomochord, and a cylindrical body composed of a proboscis, a collar, and a long trunk.

concepts.concept_title: Circulatory and Respiration System in Hemichordates
concepts.description: Hemichordates have an open circulatory system and respiration occurs through gills.
Animals belonging to phylum Chordata are characterised by the presence of a notochord, a dorsal nerve cord, and pharyngeal slits.

concepts.concept_title: Notochord Presence in Urochordata and Cephalochordata
concepts.description: Notochord is present only in larval tail in Urochordata and extends from head to tail region and is persistent throughout life in Cephalochordata.
Protochordates are exclusively marine, possess post-anal tail, and have notochord and dorsal central nervous system.
Chordates possess notochord, dorsal central nervous system, pharynx perforated by gill slits, and post-anal tail, whereas non-chordates lack these features.
Phylum Chordata is divided into subphyla Urochordata, Cephalochordata, and Vertebrata.

Vertebrates possess notochord during embryonic period, vertebral column in adulthood, ventral muscular heart with two, three or four chambers, kidneys for excretion and osmoregulation, and paired appendages.
Subphylum Vertebrata is further divided into Agnatha, Gnathostomata, Pisces, Tetrapoda, Amphibia, Reptilia, Aves, and Mammalia.
Cyclostomata are jawless vertebrates with elongated body, six to fifteen pairs of gill slits, sucking and circular mouth, and cartilaginous cranium and vertebral column.
Cartilaginous fishes possess streamlined body, cartilaginous endoskeleton, separate gill slits, and powerful jaws.
Gnathostomata are jawed vertebrates with ventrally located mouth, persistent notochord, tough skin with minute placoid scales, and backwardly directed teeth.
Bony fishes have a bony endoskeleton, streamlined body, and terminal mouth. They also have four pairs of gills, cycloid/ctenoid scales, and an air bladder.
Bony fishes reproduce externally, with some species being oviparous and others viviparous. Development is direct in most cases.
Amphibians have moist skin, lack scales, and have two pairs of limbs. They also have a three-chambered heart and external fertilization.
Amphibians reproduce externally, with sex being separate and development being indirect in most cases.
Reptiles have dry and cornified skin, epidermal scales or scutes, and a three-chambered heart. They are poikilothermic and shed their scales as skin cast.
Reptiles reproduce internally with separate sexes, and are oviparous with direct development.
Aves have feathers, beaks, and fully ossified endoskeleton. They are warm-blooded and have a four-chambered heart.
Bilateral symmetry in animals refers to the presence of identical body parts on either side of a central axis.

concepts.concept_title: Suckers and Hooks in Parasitic Forms
concepts.description: Parasitic forms of Aschelminthes exhibit distinct suckers and hooks that enable them to attach and feed on hosts.
Annelids are metamerically segmented animals with a true coelom, allowing for separate functions in each segment.
Arthropods are characterized by the presence of jointed appendages, which enable them to move and perform various functions.
Molluscs have a soft body surrounded by an external calcareous shell, providing support and protection.
Echinoderms possess a spiny skin and are characterized by the presence of a water vascular system, which aids in movement and sensory functions.
Chordates possess a notochord, a dorsal hollow nerve cord, and paired pharyngeal gill slits, which are common features among chordates.

concepts.concept_title: Dorsal Hollow Nerve Cord in Chordates
concepts.description: Chordates possess a dorsal hollow nerve cord, which serves as the precursor to the central nervous system.

concepts.concept_title: Paired Pharyngeal Gill Slits in Chordates
concepts.description: Chordates possess paired pharyngeal gill slits, which aid in respiration and are a characteristic feature among chordates.
Vertebrates possess a backbone or spinal column, while non-vertebrates do not.
Agnatha is represented by the class Cyclostomata, while Gnathostomata has two superclasses, Pisces and Tetrapoda.
Poikilothermous animals, such as fishes, amphibians, and reptiles, have a variable body temperature that is dependent on the environment.
Mammals exhibit viviparity, where the young ones develop and are nourished inside the mother's body.
Phylum Chordata includes animals that possess a notochord, a dorsal hollow nerve cord, and paired pharyngeal gill slits.

concepts.concept_title: Gross structural features of plants and animals
concepts.description: Gross structural features include both external and internal features of plants and animals that can be observed through naked eyes or magnifying lenses.
Morphology describes the external features of living organisms, while anatomy describes the internal features and their spatial relationships.

concepts.concept_title: Classification of plants based on morphological and anatomical characteristics
concepts.description: Classification of plants requires knowledge of standard technical terms and definitions, as well as variations in different parts of plants, which are adaptations to their environment.
The underground part of flowering plants is the root system, which consists of roots and is responsible for absorption of water and nutrients.
The portion of flowering plants above the ground forms the shoot system, which includes stems, leaves, flowers, and fruits.
Roots can be classified based on their structure and function. Mainly, there are two types: Tap and Fibrous roots

concepts.concept_title: Main Functions of the Root System
concepts.description: Root system is responsible for absorption of water, minerals, provision of anchorage, and synthesis of plant growth regulators
The root cap protects the root apex as it grows through the soil
The root tip has three distinct regions: Meristematic, Elongation, and Maturation zones
Epidermal cells of the elongation zone form root hairs which absorb water and minerals from the soil

concepts.concept_title: Features that Distinguish a Stem from a Root
concepts.description: A stem is the ascending part of the axis bearing branches, leaves, flowers, and fruits, whereas a root is the descending part of the axis
A stem consists of nodes, internodes, and buds which may be terminal or axillary
The main functions of the stem include conducting water, minerals, and photosynthates, storage of food, and provision of support and protection
A typical leaf consists of three main parts: lamina, leaf base, and petiole
Leaves are arranged in an acropetal order on the stem
The arrangement of veins and veinlets in the lamina of a leaf.
Reticulate and parallel are the two main types of venation.
Leaves can be simple or compound.
Pinnately compound and palmately compound are two subtypes of compound leaves.
Phyllotaxy refers to the arrangement of leaves on a stem or branch.
Alternate, opposite, and whorled are the three main types of phyllotaxy.
Inflorescence refers to the arrangement of flowers on a floral axis.
Racemose and cymose are the two main types of inflorescence.
A flower has calyx, corolla, androecium, and gynoecium as its main parts.
Flowers can be bisexual or unisexual.
A flower with only stamens or only carpels is called unisexual.
Flowers can have actinomorphic, zygomorphic, or asymmetric symmetry.
Flowers can be trimerous, tetramerous, or pentamerous based on the arrangement of floral parts.
Flowers with bracts at the base of the pedicel are called bracteate, and those without bracts are called ebracteate.
Flowers can be hypogynous, perigynous, or epigynous based on the position of the gynoecium and other floral parts.
Aestivation is the arrangement of sepals or petals in a floral bud, and can be valvate, twisted, imbricate, or vexillary.
A flower has four floral whorls: calyx, corolla, androecium, and gynoecium.
A stamen consists of a stalk (filament) and an anther, which is usually bilobed and has two pollen-sacs.
Stamens can be free (polyandrous) or united in varying degrees, forming bundles (monoadelphous, diadelphous, polyadelphous).
Gynoecium is the female reproductive part of the flower, consisting of one or more carpels, each consisting of stigma, style, and ovary.
Ovules are attached to a placenta within the ovary, and can be free (apocarpous) or fused (syncarpous).
Placentation refers to the arrangement of ovules within the ovary, types of which include marginal, axile, parietal, basal, central, and free central.
Fruit is a mature ovary developed after fertilisation, with various types including drupe, berry, and so on.
Pericarp, the wall of the fruit, can be dry or fleshy, and consists of three layers: epicarp, mesocarp, and endocarp.
A seed consists of a seed coat and an embryo, which is made up of radicle, embryonal axis, and cotyledon.
A dicotyledonous seed has two cotyledons and consists of an embryonal axis, seed coat, and hilum.

concepts.concept_title: Hilum and Micropyle in Dicotyledonous Seeds
concepts.description: Hilum is a scar on the seed coat while micropyle is a small pore present above the hilum.
Endosperm is a food storing tissue and its absence in non-endospermous seeds.
A monocotyledonous seed has a seed coat, endosperm, and embryo, with a bulky endosperm storing food.

concepts.concept_title: Aleurone Layer and Scutellum in Monocotyledonous Seeds
concepts.description: Aleurone layer is a proteinous layer separating the embryo from the endosperm, while scutellum is a large and shield-shaped cotyledon.

concepts.concept_title: Coleoptile and Coleorhiza in Monocotyledonous Seeds
concepts.description: Coleoptile and coleorhiza are sheaths enclosing the plumule and radicle, respectively.

concepts.concept_title: Semi-Technical Description of a Flowering Plant
concepts.description: A semi-technical description of a flowering plant involves describing its habit, vegetative characters, floral characters, and presenting a floral diagram and floral formula.
A floral diagram shows the number of flower parts, their arrangement, and the relation between them, while a floral formula represents the floral structure using symbols.
The Brassicaceae family is commonly called the potato family, distributed in tropics, subtropics, and temperate zones.
Solanaceae family plants are mostly herbs, shrubs, or small trees with herbaceous stems, alternate simple leaves, and underground stems in potato.
Phyllotaxy refers to the arrangement of leaves on the stem. It can be either alternate, opposite, or whorled
A pinnately compound leaf is a leaf that is divided into a central axis with leaflets arranged on either side
Valvate aestivation refers to the arrangement of sepals or petals in a flower, where they overlap in such a way that they form valves that open outward
Epipetalous stamens are stamens that arise from the petals or the petaloid structures in a flower
Bicarpellary obligately placed gynoecium refers to the gynoecium of a flower with two fused carpels, where the ovaries are placed adjacent to each other
Reticulate venation refers to the characteristic pattern of veins found in leaves, where the veins form a net-like arrangement
Fibrous roots refer to the type of root system found in monocotyledonous plants, consisting of multiple, smaller rootlets arising from the base of the stem
Inflorescence refers to the arrangement of flowers on a stem or branch, which can take various forms such as racemose, cymose, or solitary
Placentation refers to the arrangement of ovules within the ovary of a flower, which can be axile, parietal, or basal
Floral characters refer to the morphological features of a flower that contribute to its identification and classification
Floral formula is a shorthand representation of the morphological features of a flower, which helps in the classification and identification of flowering plants
Study of internal structure of plants is called anatomy.
Based on structure and location, there are three types of tissue systems: epidermal, ground or fundamental, and vascular or conducting tissue systems.
Forms the outer-most covering of the whole plant body, consisting of epidermal cells, stomata, and epidermal appendages.
Parenchymatous cells with a small amount of cytoplasm and a large vacuole, forming a continuous layer.
Regulate the process of transpiration and gaseous exchange, consisting of guard cells and a stomatal pore.
Bean- or dumb-bell-shaped cells that regulate opening and closing of stomata, possessing chloroplasts.
Specialised cells surrounding guard cells, aiding in stomatal function.
Single-celled or multicellular structures on epidermal cells, aiding in preventing water loss.
Comprises simple tissues such as parenchyma, collenchyma, and sclerenchyma, present in cortex, pericycle, and pith.
Responsible for conducting water, minerals, and nutrients throughout the plant body.

concepts.concept_title: Xylem and Phloem Tissues in Vascular System
concepts.description: Xylem and phloem are complex tissues that constitute vascular bundles.
Vascular bundles can be open or closed, radial or conjoint.
Cambium is present in open vascular bundles and enables secondary growth.
