A dicot root has epidermis, cortex, endodermis, pericycle, and vascular bundles.
Root hairs and intercellular spaces are present in the cortex.
Conjunctive tissue is present between xylem and phloem, and forms the stele.
A monocot root has epidermis, cortex, endodermis, pericycle, and vascular bundles.

concepts.concept_title: Difference Between Dicot and Monocot Roots
concepts.description: Dicots have fewer xylem bundles, while monocots have more xylem bundles.
A dicot stem has epidermis, cortex, and pericycle.
Endodermis is a layer rich in starch, also referred to as the starch sheath. It is present on the inner side of the endodermis.
The pericycle is a layer of semi-lunar patches of sclerenchyma cells above the phloem.

concepts.concept_title: Radial Parenchymatous Cells and Medullary Rays
concepts.description: Radially placed parenchymatous cells constitute medullary rays and are present between vascular bundles.

concepts.concept_title: Ring Arrangement of Vascular Bundles in Dicot Stem
concepts.description: A large number of vascular bundles arranged in a ring is a characteristic of a dicot stem.
Each vascular bundle in a dicot stem has endarch protoxylem.
Pith is a large number of rounded, parenchymatous cells with large intercellular spaces occupying the central portion of the stem.
Monocot stem has a sclerenchymatous hypodermis.

concepts.concept_title: Scattered Vascular Bundles in Monocot Stem
concepts.description: Monocot stem has a large number of scattered vascular bundles.
Each vascular bundle in a monocot stem is surrounded by a sclerenchymatous bundle sheath.
Monocot stem has a large, conspicuous parenchymatous ground tissue.
Phloem parenchyma is absent in monocot stems.

concepts.concept_title: Water-Containing Cavities in Monocot Vascular Bundles
concepts.description: Water-containing cavities are present within vascular bundles of monocot stems.

concepts.concept_title: Differentiation of Epidermis in Dorsiventral Leaf
concepts.description: A dorsiventral leaf has a differentiated epidermis with adaxial and abaxial sides.

concepts.concept_title: Epidermal Cuticle and Stoma Density in Dorsiventral Leaf
concepts.description: Adaxial epidermis may lack stomata, while abaxial epidermis generally bears more stomata. Cuticle is present on both epidermal surfaces.

concepts.concept_title: Mesophyll Structure and Photosynthesis in Dorsiventral Leaf
concepts.description: Mesophyll in a dorsiventral leaf consists of chloroplast-containing parenchyma cells, which perform photosynthesis.
Palisade parenchyma is a layer of vertically arranged, elongated cells with chloroplasts.
Spongy parenchyma is a layer of loosely arranged, oval or round cells with air cavities.

concepts.concept_title: Vascular Bundles and Venation in Monocot Leaves
concepts.description: Monocot leaves have conjoint and closed vascular bundles with parallel venation.

concepts.concept_title: Bulliform Cells and Water Absorption in Monocot Leaves
concepts.description: Adaxial epidermal cells in monocot leaves can modify into bulliform cells, which absorb water and cause leaf movement.

concepts.concept_title: Division of Labour in Cells, Tissues, Organs, and Organ Systems
concepts.description: Cells, tissues, organs, and organ systems exhibit division of labour by splitting work to ensure the survival of the body.
Tissues are groups of similar cells with intercellular substances performing specific functions in the body.
There are four basic types of tissues in complex animals: epithelial, connective, muscular, and neural.
Organs are formed by the organisation of tissues, and organ systems are formed by the connection of two or more organs to perform a common function.

concepts.concept_title: Evolutionary Trend in Organ and Organ Systems
concepts.description: There is a discernable trend in the complexity of organs and organ systems, known as evolutionary trend, which will be studied in detail in class XII.
Morphology is the study of the form or externally visible features of an animal, which includes the study of the external appearance of organs or parts of the body.
Anatomy is the study of the morphology of internal organs in animals, which is conventional in the field of zoology.
Frogs are cold-blooded animals, also known as poikilotherms, whose body temperature varies with the temperature of the environment.
Frogs have the ability to change their skin colour to hide from their enemies, known as camouflage or mimicry.
Frogs undergo summer sleep (aestivation) and winter sleep (hibernation) to protect themselves from extreme heat and cold.
The body of a frog is divisible into head, trunk, forelimbs, and hindlimbs. The head has nostrils and eyes, while the forelimbs end in four digits and the hindlimbs in five digits with webbed feet.
The mouth opens into the buccal cavity, which leads to the oesophagus through the pharynx, and the alimentary canal is short due to the carnivorous diet.
The digestive system consists of alimentary canal and digestive glands; the liver secretes bile stored in the gall bladder, and the pancreas produces pancreatic juice containing digestive enzymes.

Digestion takes place by the action of HCl and gastric juices in the stomach, followed by the emulsification of fat and digestion of carbohydrates and proteins in the intestine using bile and pancreatic juices.
Frogs respire by skin (cutaneous respiration) in water and by buccal cavity, skin, and lungs (pulmonary respiration) on land.
The frog's heart is a muscular structure with three chambers, while the blood vascular system is a well-developed closed type including the heart, blood vessels, and blood.
Male frogs have sound-producing vocal sacs and a copulatory pad on the first digit of the forelimbs, distinguishing them from female frogs.
Frogs have a venous system, which includes the hepatic portal system and the renal portal system.
Blood is composed of plasma and cells, which include red blood cells (RBCs), white blood cells (WBCs), and platelets.
Blood and lymph differ in protein content and cell presence.
Blood carries nutrients, gases, and water to respective sites during circulation.
The muscular heart pumps blood to achieve circulation.
The excretory system, consisting of kidneys, ureters, cloaca, and urinary bladder, eliminates nitrogenous wastes.
Each kidney is composed of several structural and functional units called uriniferous tubules or nephrons.
Frogs excrete urea and are ureotelic animals.
Blood carries waste to the kidneys, which separate and excrete it.
Frogs have a well-evolved nervous system and endocrine glands for control and coordination.
The nervous system of frogs includes the central nervous system (CNS) and peripheral nervous system (PNS).
Frogs have various endocrine glands, including pituitary, thyroid, parathyroid, thymus, pineal body, pancreas, adrenal glands, and gonads.
The nervous system is organized into CNS, PNS, and autonomic nervous system (ANS).
The brain is divided into fore-brain, mid-brain, and hind-brain, and is enclosed in a bony structure called cranium.
Frogs have various sense organs, including organs of touch, taste, smell, vision, and hearing.
Frogs have well-organized male and female reproductive systems, including testes, ovaries, and associated structures.
The female reproductive organs of frogs include two ovaries that release ova into the oviduct, which opens into the cloaca.
Frogs undergo external fertilisation, and a mature female can lay 2500-3000 ova at a time.
Frogs have a life cycle consisting of egg, tadpole, and adult stages, with tadpoles undergoing metamorphosis to form adults.
Frogs have a body covered by skin, with mucous glands and are highly vascularised for respiration in water and on land.
The digestive system of a frog consists of the alimentary canal and major digestive glands like the liver and pancreas.
The urinary system of frogs consists of the kidneys and urinogenital ducts that open into the cloaca.
Male frogs have testes, and female frogs have ovaries, with eggs hatching into tadpoles and metamorphosing into adults.

concepts.concept_title: Circulatory and Respiratory Systems of Frogs
concepts.description: Frogs have a closed circulatory system with single circulation and can respire in water through skin and on land through lungs.
Frogs have a nervous system organised into central, peripheral, and autonomic systems.
Cell theory is based on the idea that all living organisms are composed of cells.
The cell is the basic unit of life in all living organisms.
Cellular organisation of all life forms emphasizes unity underlying diversity of forms.
A physico-chemical approach to study and understand living organisms.
Polymers of biological origin, such as proteins, nucleic acids, etc.
The study of allowed conformations of proteins and other biopolymers.
The study of the structure and function of biological macromolecules.
The triple helical structure of collagen and its significance.
Cell theory states that all living organisms are composed of cells and their products, and new cells are formed from pre-existing cells.
Cells are classified into eukaryotic and prokaryotic based on the presence or absence of a membrane-bound nucleus.
A thin outer layer that delimits the cell and maintains its shape and structure, also called the plasma membrane.
A rigid layer that provides support and shape to plant cells, absent in animal cells.
A dense membrane-bound structure containing chromosomes and DNA, found in eukaryotic cells, absent in prokaryotic cells.
A semi-fluid matrix that occupies the cell and is the main arena of cellular activities.
Specialized membrane-bound structures found in eukaryotic cells, such as endoplasmic reticulum, golgi complex, and mitochondria.
Non-membrane bound organelles found in all cells, responsible for protein synthesis.

concepts.concept_title: Shape and Size Variability of Cells
concepts.description: Cells differ greatly in size and shape depending on their function and type.
Prokaryotic cells are smaller, multiply rapidly, and lack a true nucleus and organelles.
Prokaryotic cells have a cell wall surrounding the cell membrane, except in mycoplasma, and a three-layered cell envelope consisting of glycocalyx, cell wall, and plasma membrane.

A specialized differentiated form of cell membrane called mesosome, which is essentially infoldings of cell membrane, is characteristic of prokaryotes and helps in cell wall formation, DNA replication, and distribution to daughter cells.
Bacteria can be classified into Gram positive and Gram negative based on the differences in their cell envelopes and the manner in which they respond to the staining procedure developed by Gram.
The cell envelope of bacteria consists of glycocalyx, cell wall, and plasma membrane, and performs distinct functions, but acts together as a single protective unit.
Bacteria may be motile or non-motile, and if motile, they have thin filamentous extensions from their cell wall called flagella, which are composed of three parts – filament, hook, and basal body.
Besides flagella, Pili and Fimbriae are also surface structures of bacteria, which help in various processes.

concepts.concept_title: Fluid Mosaic Model of Cell Membrane
concepts.description: The fluid mosaic model describes the structure of the cell membrane as a fluid bilayer with lipids and embedded proteins.
Passive transport is the movement of molecules across the cell membrane without the use of energy, such as simple diffusion and osmosis.
Active transport is the movement of molecules across the cell membrane against their concentration gradient, requiring the use of energy in the form of ATP.
The cell wall provides structural support, protection, and barrier function to plant cells, and its composition varies among different cell types.
The endomembrane system comprises a network of membranous organelles including ER, Golgi complex, lysosomes, and vacuoles, which work together to perform various cellular functions.

The endoplasmic reticulum is a network of membranous tubular structures scattered in the cytoplasm, divided into luminal and extra-luminal compartments, and distinguished as rough and smooth ER based on the presence or absence of ribosomes.
RER is frequently observed in cells actively involved in protein synthesis and secretion.
The golgi apparatus performs the function of packaging materials, to be delivered either to the intra-cellular targets or secreted outside the cell.
The golgi apparatus consists of flat, disc-shaped sacs or cisternae of 0.5µm to 1.0µm diameter, stacked parallel to each other.
The cis and the trans faces of the golgi apparatus are entirely different, but interconnected.
Lysosomes are membrane-bound vesicular structures formed by the process of packaging in the golgi apparatus.
Lysosomes are rich in hydrolytic enzymes that are capable of digesting carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids.
Vacuoles are membrane-bound spaces that contain water, sap, excretory products, and other materials not useful for the cell.
The tonoplast facilitates the transport of ions and other materials against concentration gradients into the vacuole.
Mitochondria are double membrane-bound structures with the outer membrane and the inner membrane dividing its lumen distinctly into two aqueous compartments.
The inner compartment of the mitochondrion is filled with a dense homogeneous substance called the matrix, and the outer membrane forms the continuous limiting boundary of the organelle.
The inner membrane of the mitochondrion forms a number of infoldings called the cristae towards the matrix.
Mitochondria are double membrane bound organelles responsible for aerobic respiration, producing ATP as cellular energy.
Plastids are classified into chloroplasts, chromoplasts and leucoplasts based on the type of pigments they contain.
Chloroplasts are double membrane bound organelles containing thylakoids, grana, and stroma lamellae.
Chloroplasts are responsible for trapping light energy for photosynthesis.
Ribosomes are composed of RNA and proteins, and have two subunits: 60S and 40S for 80S ribosomes and 50S and 30S for 70S ribosomes.
There are two types of ribosomes: 80S (eukaryotic) and 70S (prokaryotic).
Cytoskeleton is an elaborate network of filamentous proteinaceous structures consisting of microtubules, microfilaments, and intermediate filaments.
Cilia and flagella are hair-like structures composed of microtubules, covered by plasma membrane, and are involved in cell movement.
Axoneme is a core structure of cilia and flagella composed of 9 doublets of peripheral microtubules and 2 central microtubules.
Axonemal microtubules are arranged in a 9+2 array, with doublets connected by bridges and spokes.
Pericentriolar materials are amorphous regions surrounding centrioles in the centrosome.
Centrioles are cylindrical structures in the centrosome, composed of 9 evenly spaced peripheral fibrils of tubulin protein.
Centrioles form the basal bodies of cilia or flagella, and spindle fibers that give rise to spindle apparatus.
Nuclear envelope consists of two parallel membranes with a space between called the perinuclear space.
Nuclear pores are formed by fusion of nuclear membrane layers and are passages for RNA and protein movement.
Nucleus contains nucleolus and chromatin, and is involved in ribosomal RNA synthesis.
Nucleolus is a site for active ribosomal RNA synthesis and contains nucleoplasm.
Nucleoli are present in cells actively carrying out protein synthesis.
Chromatin contains DNA and histones, with some non-histone proteins and RNA, forming a loose and indistinct network.
A single human cell has approximately two-meter long DNA distributed among its forty-six chromosomes.
The centromere is the primary constriction on a chromosome where the kinetochores are present.
Kinetochores are disc-shaped structures on the sides of the centromere where microtubules attach during cell division.
Chromosomes can be classified into metacentric, submetacentric, acrocentric, and telocentric based on the position of the centromere.
Some chromosomes have non-staining secondary constrictions at a constant location, giving the appearance of a small fragment called a satellite.
Microbodies are membrane-bound minute vesicles containing enzymes, present in both plant and animal cells.
Cells can be classified as eukaryotic or prokaryotic based on the presence or absence of a membrane-bound nucleus and other organelles.
A typical eukaryotic cell consists of a cell membrane, nucleus, and cytoplasm, with plant cells having a cell wall outside the cell membrane.
The endomembrane system includes ER, golgi complex, lysosomes, and vacuoles, working together to perform different functions.
All cell organelles perform specific functions, including protein synthesis, transport, and digestion.
The nucleus controls the activities of organelles and plays a major role in heredity, containing nucleoli and chromatin network.
Centrosome and centriole form the basal body of cilia and flagella, facilitating locomotion, and also form spindle apparatus during cell division.
Endoplasmic reticulum contains tubules or cisternae, with two types: rough and smooth, involved in the transport of substances and synthesis of proteins.
The golgi body is a membranous organelle composed of flattened sacs, involved in the packing and transportation of secretions.
Lysosomes are single membrane structures containing enzymes for the digestion of all types of macromolecules.
Ribosomes are involved in protein synthesis, occurring freely in the cytoplasm or associated with ER.
Mitochondria help in oxidative phosphorylation and generation of adenosine triphosphate, bounded by a double membrane.
Plastids are pigment-containing organelles found in plant cells, responsible for trapping light energy essential for photosynthesis.
Chloroplasts are green-colored plastids containing chlorophyll, responsible for trapping light energy essential for photosynthesis.

concepts.concept_title: Cell as the Structural and Functional Unit of Life
concepts.description: Cells are the basic structural and functional units of life.
Plastids are chromoplasts that contain pigments like carotene and xanthophyll.
Robert Brown discovered the cell, while Schleiden and Schwann formulated the cell theory, and Virchow explained that cells are formed from pre-existing cells.
New cells generate from pre-existing cells, not from bacterial fermentation, regeneration of old cells, or abiotic materials.
Thylakoids are disc-shaped sacs in chloroplasts and are involved in photosynthesis.
Cristae and cisternae are infoldings of the mitochondrial inner membrane, with cisternae often found as flat membranous sacs in stroma and cristae as infoldings in mitochondria.
Nucleus is enclosed by the nuclear envelope, a double membrane structure with nuclear pores.
Mitochondria are double membrane-bound organelles with a highly folded inner membrane.
Prokaryotic cells lack membrane-bound organelles and have a single circular DNA molecule.
Lysosomes and vacuoles are endomembrane structures with different functions.
Nuclear pores are involved in the exchange of genetic material between nucleus and cytoplasm. Centromere is an essential region of the chromosome for proper segregation.

concepts.concept_title: Solute Movement Across the Plasma Membrane
concepts.description: Neutral solutes can move across the plasma membrane, while polar molecules move through facilitated diffusion with the help of specific transport proteins.
Multicellular organisms exhibit cell division and division of labor.
Elemental analysis is performed on a sample of living tissue to obtain a list of elements and their respective content per unit mass.

concepts.concept_title: Comparison of Elements in Living and Non-living Matter
concepts.description: There is no significant difference in the list of elements present in living and non-living matter, but the relative abundance of elements may vary.
All carbon compounds obtained from living tissues can be called 'biomolecules', which can be isolated and purified using analytical techniques.

concepts.concept_title: Detection of Inorganic Elements in Living Tissues
concepts.description: Inorganic elements in living tissues can be detected by burning a sample and observing the ash, which contains inorganic elements and compounds.
Elemental analysis gives the elemental composition of living tissues in the form of hydrogen, oxygen, chlorine, carbon, etc.
Amino acids are organic compounds containing an amino group and an acidic group as substituents on the same carbon.
The R group in amino acids is a variable group that determines the nature of the amino acid, and it can be different in different amino acids.
In solutions of different pH, the structure of amino acids changes, and they can exist in a zwitterionic form.

concepts.concept_title: Chemical and Physical Properties of Amino Acids
concepts.description: The chemical and physical properties of amino acids are determined by their amino, carboxyl, and R functional groups.
Amino acids can be classified into acidic, basic, and neutral amino acids based on the number of amino and carboxyl groups.
A fatty acid has a carboxyl group attached to an R group, which can be a methyl, ethyl, or other groups.
Fatty acids can be saturated or unsaturated, depending on the presence of double bonds.
Glycerol is a trihydroxy propane, and it can esterified with fatty acids to form monoglycerides, diglycerides, and triglycerides.
Nitrogen bases, such as adenine, guanine, cytosine, uracil, and thymine, are found in nucleic acids and are essential for their structure and function.
Nucleosides are formed when nitrogen bases are attached to a sugar, and nucleotides are formed when a phosphate group is also attached.

concepts.concept_title: Role of Nucleic Acids in Living Organisms
concepts.description: Nucleic acids, such as DNA and RNA, function as genetic material and store genetic information.
Biomolecules can be classified into micromolecules (molecular weight < 1000 Da) and macromolecules (molecular weight ≥ 1000 Da).
Primary metabolites have identifiable functions and play known roles in normal physiological processes. Secondary metabolites do not have understood roles or functions in host organisms.

concepts.concept_title: Molecular Weight of Organic Compounds in Acid Soluble and Insoluble Fraction
concepts.description: Compounds in acid soluble pool have molecular weights < 800 Da. Compounds in acid insoluble fraction have molecular weights ≥ 1000 Da, except for lipids which have molecular weights < 800 Da.
Biomacromolecules, except for lipids, are polymeric substances. Lipids, despite having small molecular weights, are present in macromolecular fraction due to their association with other structures.
Water is the most abundant chemical in living organisms, followed by proteins, carbohydrates, nucleic acids, and lipids.

concepts.concept_title: Classification of Biomolecules based on Molecular Weight
concepts.description: Biomolecules can be classified into micromolecules (molecular weight < 1000 Da) and macromolecules (molecular weight ≥ 1000 Da).
DNA contains deoxyribose sugar while RNA contains ribose sugar. DNA contains adenine, thymine, cytosine, and guanine while RNA contains adenine, uracil, cytosine, and guanine.
Primary structure refers to the sequence of amino acids in a protein. It is also known as the positional information of a protein.
Secondary structure refers to the local arrangement of amino acids in a protein. It includes alpha helices and beta pleated sheets.
Tertiary structure refers to the overall 3D arrangement of amino acids in a protein. It is responsible for the biological activities of proteins.
Quaternary structure refers to the arrangement of multiple polypeptide subunits in a protein. It includes the architecture of a protein.
An active site is a crevice or pocket on an enzyme where the substrate binds and catalysis occurs.
Some enzymes from thermophilic organisms can retain their catalytic power at higher temperatures.
A change in shape without breaking of bonds is a physical process. Breaking and forming new bonds during transformation is a chemical reaction.

concepts.concept_title: Rate of a Physical or Chemical Process
concepts.description: The rate of a process refers to the amount of product formed per unit time, denoted as δP/δt.
The rate of a process doubles or decreases by half for every 10°C change in temperature.
Catalysed reactions proceed at rates vastly higher than uncatalysed ones.
Enzyme-catalysed reactions have much higher rates than uncatalysed reactions.
A multistep chemical reaction, when each step is catalysed by enzymes, is called a metabolic pathway.
The active site of an enzyme is a region that binds substrates and catalyzes chemical reactions.
The formation of an enzyme-substrate complex is an obligatory step in enzyme catalysis.
The transition state is a temporary structure formed during enzyme catalysis, where the substrate is converted into product.
The unstable intermediate state between the substrate and product in a reaction, where the reaction energy reaches its maximum.
The energy difference between the substrate and transition state, representing the energy barrier that must be overcome for a reaction to occur.
A reaction is exothermic when it releases energy, and endothermic when it requires energy to proceed, with the activation energy determining the reaction type.
A short-lived complex formed when the substrate binds to the enzyme, essential for catalysis, and leading to the formation of product(s) and the recovery of the enzyme.

The series of steps involved in enzyme action, including substrate binding, shape alteration, bond breaking, and product release, allowing the enzyme to bind to another substrate and repeat the cycle.
The specific temperature and pH range at which an enzyme functions optimally, with activity declining below or above these values.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Substrate Concentration on Enzyme Activity
concepts.description: The increase in substrate concentration initially increases the velocity of the enzymatic reaction, reaching a maximum velocity before saturation, with enzymes being outnumbred by substrate molecules.
The binding of specific chemicals to the enzyme can affect its activity, either by inhibiting its function or by activating it, depending on the chemical and enzyme involved.
Competitive inhibition occurs when an enzyme's activity is inhibited by a molecule (inhibitor) that closely resembles the substrate and competes with it for the enzyme's active site.

There are two types of enzyme inhibition: competitive (where the inhibitor competes with the substrate for the active site) and non-competitive (where the inhibitor binds to a different site on the enzyme).
Enzymes are classified into 6 main classes based on the type of reactions they catalyse: oxidoreductases, transferases, hydrolases, lyases, isomerases, and ligases.
Oxidoreductases are enzymes that catalyse oxidation-reduction reactions between two substrates, resulting in the transfer of electrons from one substrate to another.
Transferases are enzymes that catalyse the transfer of a group (other than hydrogen) between two substrates, resulting in the formation of a new bond.
Hydrolases are enzymes that catalyse the hydrolysis of ester, ether, peptide, glycosidic, C-C, C-halide, or P-N bonds.
Lyases are enzymes that catalyse the removal of a group from a substrate, resulting in the formation of a double bond.
Isomerases are enzymes that catalyse the interconversion of optical, geometric, or positional isomers.
Ligases are enzymes that catalyse the linking together of two compounds, resulting in the formation of a new covalent bond.
Co-factors are non-protein constituents that are bound to an enzyme to make it catalytically active, including prosthetic groups, co-enzymes, and metal ions.
Prosthetic groups are organic compounds that are tightly bound to the apoenzyme and are part of the active site.
Co-enzymes are organic compounds that associate with the apoenzyme during catalysis, often containing vitamins as essential chemical components.
Metal ions can act as co-factors for enzymes, forming coordination bonds with the enzyme's active site and substrates.
Macromolecules are large biomolecules composed of smaller units. There are four types: proteins, nucleic acids, polysaccharides, and lipids.
Biomacromolecules have a hierarchical structure consisting of primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary levels.
Proteins can function as enzymes, which catalyze biochemical reactions. Enzymes exhibit substrate specificity, require optimal temperature and pH, and are denatured at high temperatures.
Nucleic acids store and transmit genetic information from one generation to the next.

concepts.concept_title: Polysaccharides as Storage Forms of Energy
concepts.description: Polysaccharides like starch and glycogen serve as storage forms of energy in living organisms.

concepts.concept_title: Lipids as Components of Cellular Membranes
concepts.description: Lipids, such as phospholipids, are components of cellular membranes due to their association with membranes.
Enzymes exhibit important properties like lowering activation energy, enhancing reaction rates, and requiring specific conditions for maximal activity.
Ribozymes are nucleic acids with catalytic power, similar to enzymes.
Proteins serve various functions in cells, including acting as enzymes, antibodies, receptors, hormones, and structural proteins.
The tertiary structure of proteins refers to the three-dimensional arrangement of amino acids in a protein.
The sequence of events in which a cell duplicates its genome, synthesizes other cell constituents, and eventually divides into two daughter cells.
A cell cycle consists of two basic phases: interphase and M phase (mitosis). Interphase is further divided into G1, S, and G2 phases.
The phase between two successive M phases during which cell growth and DNA replication occur.
The phase of cell division in which chromosomes are separated and cytoplasm is divided.
The phase between the end of mitosis and the beginning of DNA replication where the cell is actively growing but not replicating its DNA.
The phase where DNA synthesis or replication takes place, resulting in an increase in the amount of DNA per cell.
The phase between the end of DNA replication and the beginning of mitosis, where the cell is preparing for cell division.
Mitotic cell division is typically seen in diploid somatic cells of animals.
Haploid cells can divide by mitosis in plants, in contrast to animals.
In plants, mitosis is observed in both haploid and diploid cells, especially during the alternation of generations.
The cell cycle has three main phases: S (DNA replication), G (growing), and M (mitosis) phases.
Chromosomal material undergoes condensation during prophase, resulting in compact mitotic chromosomes.
The centrosome duplication and movement towards opposite poles of the cell form the mitotic apparatus.
The complete disintegration of the nuclear envelope marks the start of the metaphase.
The morphology of chromosomes is most easily studied during metaphase.
Karyokinesis involves the four stages of nuclear division: prophase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase.
In metaphase, chromosomes align at the equator of the cell and are attached to spindle fibers through kinetochores.
The plane of alignment of the chromosomes at metaphase is referred to as the metaphase plate.
In anaphase, the centromeres split and daughter chromatids separate, moving towards the opposite poles.
During anaphase, sister chromatids separate and move to opposite poles.
In telophase, chromosomes reach the poles, decondense, and lose individuality, eventually forming daughter nuclei.
During telophase, a nuclear envelope develops around the chromosome clusters at each pole, forming daughter nuclei.
Cytokinesis is the division of the cytoplasm, resulting in the formation of two daughter cells.
In animal cells, cytokinesis occurs through the formation of a furrow in the plasma membrane.
In plant cells, cytokinesis occurs through the formation of a new cell wall, starting from the center and growing outward.

concepts.concept_title: Mitosis Results in Diploid Daughter Cells
concepts.description: Mitosis leads to the production of diploid daughter cells with identical genetic complements.

concepts.concept_title: Growth of Multicellular Organisms Due to Mitosis
concepts.description: The growth of multicellular organisms is due to mitosis, which restores the nucleo-cytoplasmic ratio.

concepts.concept_title: Significance of Mitosis in Cell Repair
concepts.description: Mitosis plays a crucial role in cell repair by replacing damaged cells in the upper layer of the epidermis, gut lining, and blood cells.
Meiosis results in the production of haploid daughter cells with a complete haploid set of chromosomes.

concepts.concept_title: Meiosis Ensures Haploid Phase in Organisms
concepts.description: Meiosis ensures the production of haploid phase in the life cycle of sexually reproducing organisms.
Meiosis involves two sequential cycles of nuclear and cell division, pairing of homologous chromosomes, and recombination between non-sister chromatids.
Meiosis can be divided into the following phases: Meiosis I and Meiosis II, with respective prophase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase stages.
Prophase I in meiosis is characterized by the pairing of homologous chromosomes, the formation of recombination nodules, and crossing over between non-sister chromatids.

concepts.concept_title: Synapsis and Tetrad Formation in Prophase I
concepts.description: Synapsis is the pairing of homologous chromosomes during prophase I, resulting in the formation of a bivalent or tetrad.
Crossing over is the exchange of genetic material between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes during meiosis, mediated by the enzyme recombinase.
Chiasmata are X-shaped structures formed at the sites of crossing over between homologous chromosomes.
Terminalisation of chiasmata is the process by which chiasmata is formed at the end of diplotene, marking the transition to diakinesis.
Diakinesis is the final stage of meiotic prophase I, marked by the disappearance of nucleolus, dissolution of nuclear envelope and assembly of meiotic spindle.
Metaphase I is the stage of meiosis I where homologous chromosomes align at the equator, with microtubules attached to kinetochores.
Anaphase I is the stage of meiosis I where homologous chromosomes separate, while sister chromatids remain associated at their centromeres.
Telophase I is the stage of meiosis I where the nuclear membrane and nucleolus reappear, and cytokinesis follows, resulting in the formation of a dyad of cells.
Interkinesis is the stage between the two meiotic divisions, where there is no replication of DNA and it is a short-lived stage.
Prophase II is the stage of meiosis II, where the nuclear membrane disappears and chromosomes become compact.
Metaphase II is the stage of meiosis II where chromosomes align at the equator, with microtubules attached to kinetochores.
Anaphase II is the stage of meiosis II, where the centromere splits and sister chromatids move to opposite poles of the cell.
Telophase II is the final stage of meiosis II, where nuclei reappear and cytokinesis follows, resulting in the formation of haploid daughter cells.
The cell cycle consists of interphase and mitosis, comprising G1, S, and G2 phases. Interphase is divided into G1 and G2 phases.
Mitosis is divided into four stages: prophase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase, where chromosomes condense, align, separate, and reorganize.
Meiosis I consists of leptotene, zygotene, pachytene, diplotene, and diakinesis phases, where homologous chromosomes pair and undergo crossing over.
Meiosis I and II are two separate divisions, where homologous chromosomes separate and sister chromatids separate during meiosis II, respectively.
Cytokinesis is the process of cytoplasmic division following mitosis and meiosis, resulting in the formation of two daughter cells.
Meiosis is the reduction division that results in the halving of the chromosome number, while mitosis is the equational division that conserves the chromosome number.
During meiosis II, sister chromatids separate, resulting in the formation of four haploid cells.

DNA replication is the process by which a cell makes an exact copy of its DNA before cell division. This process involves unwinding of the double helix, synthesis of new DNA strands, and repair mechanisms to ensure accuracy.
Replication enzymes, including helicase and primase, are essential for DNA replication. Helicase unwinds the double helix, while primase synthesizes RNA primers to initiate DNA synthesis.
SOS repair mechanisms are a set of emergency measures to repair DNA damage, including the activation of repair pathways and the synthesis of repair enzymes.
Proofreading and editing are mechanisms that check for errors during DNA replication and repair. This ensures the stability and integrity of the genetic material.
Photosynthesis is a process by which green plants make their own food and produce oxygen as a by-product.
Photosynthesis is the primary source of all food on earth and responsible for the release of oxygen into the atmosphere.
The light reaction transforms light energy into chemical energy and involves the involvement of pigments and electron transport.
The photosynthetic machinery in plants involves the presence of chlorophyll, other pigments and various complexes that help in the transformation of light energy.
ATP and NADPH are high-energy molecules produced during the photosynthesis process and are used in subsequent reactions.
The Calvin cycle is a series of reactions in which CO2 is fixed into glucose, using CO2 from the atmosphere and energy from ATP and NADPH.
CO is essential for photosynthesis as plants require it for the process to occur.

concepts.concept_title: Starch Formation and photosynthesis in green parts of leaves
concepts.description: Green parts of leaves produce starch in the presence of light, whereas the non-green parts do not undergo photosynthesis.
Priestley's experiment showed that plants purify the air by releasing oxygen, while breathing animals and burning candles consume oxygen.
Sunlight is essential for photosynthesis as shown by Jan Ingenhousz's experiment with aquatic plants producing oxygen bubbles in the presence of sunlight.
Glucose is produced in the green parts of plants and is usually stored as starch, as demonstrated by JULIUS VON SACHS' experiment.
The action spectrum of photosynthesis is similar to the absorption spectra of chlorophyll a and b, as observed by T.W. ENGELMANN's experiment.
Photosynthesis is essentially a light-dependent reaction in which hydrogen from a suitable oxidizable compound reduces carbon dioxide to carbohydrates.
The empirical equation 6CO + 12H2O → C6H12O6 + 6H2O + 6O2 represents the overall process of photosynthesis.
The oxygen released in photosynthesis comes from water, not carbon dioxide.
The chloroplast has a membranous system consisting of grana, stroma lamellae, and the matrix stroma, and is responsible for trapping light energy and synthesizing ATP and NADPH.
The light reactions are directly light-driven, while the dark reactions are dependent on the products of light reactions (ATP and NADPH).
Photosynthesis involves multiple types of pigments, which can be separated using paper chromatography.
Chloroplasts align themselves with their flat surfaces parallel to the walls of mesophyll cells to receive optimum light.
Photosynthesis occurs not only in the green leaf but also in other green parts of the plant.
Pigments absorb light at specific wavelengths and can be divided into photoactive chromoproteins (chlorophyll, etc.) and accessory pigments.

concepts.concept_title: Wavelength of Maximum Absorption by Chlorophyll a
concepts.description: The wavelength at which chlorophyll a shows maximum absorption is between the blue and red regions of the visible spectrum.
Carotenoids protect chlorophyll a from photo-oxidation and enable a wider range of wavelengths to be utilised for photosynthesis.
Light reactions include light absorption, water splitting, oxygen release, and the formation of high-energy chemical intermediates (ATP and NADPH).
Pigments are organised into two discrete photochemical light harvesting complexes (LHC) within Photosystem I (PS I) and Photosystem II (PS II).

concepts.concept_title: Components of the Light Harvesting Complex
concepts.description: The light harvesting complex is made up of hundreds of pigment molecules bound to proteins, forming a light harvesting system also called antennae.
The reaction centre is different in both Photosystem I and Photosystem II, with chlorophyll a molecules having absorption peaks at 700 nm (P700) and 680 nm (P680), respectively.
ATP synthesis through light-dependent reactions in thylakoids using chemiosmosis.
Protons accumulate in the thylakoid lumen due to water splitting, electron transport, and NADP+ reductase enzyme.
Proton gradient develops across the thylakoid membrane due to differential proton concentration between stroma and thylakoid lumen.
ATP synthesis through chemiosmosis requires a proton gradient, ATP synthase, and a transmembrane channel.

concepts.concept_title: Role of ATP Synthase in Photosynthesis
concepts.description: ATP synthase breaks down the proton gradient to synthesize ATP.

concepts.concept_title: Importance of Proton Gradient in ATP Synthesis
concepts.description: The breakdown of the proton gradient provides energy for ATP synthesis.

concepts.concept_title: Location of ATP and NADPH Utilization
concepts.description: ATP and NADPH are used in the biosynthetic phase of photosynthesis in the stroma to synthesize sugars.
NADPH is used in the biosynthetic phase of photosynthesis for various reactions.
Melvin Calvin discovered the Calvin cycle using radioactive 14C in algal photosynthesis studies and identified the first product of CO fixation as 3-phosphoglyceric acid (PGA).
CO assimilation during photosynthesis is of two main types: C pathway and C pathway, based on the first product of CO fixation in different plants.
The primary acceptor molecule of CO is a 5-carbon ketose sugar – ribulose bisphosphate (RuBP).
The Calvin pathway occurs in all photosynthetic plants and operates in a cyclic manner, regenerating RuBP and producing sugar.
Carboxylation is the fixation of CO into a stable organic intermediate and is catalysed by RuBP carboxylase-oxygenase (RuBisCO), resulting in the formation of 3-PGA.
The Calvin cycle's carboxylation stage involves the combination of CO2 with ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate.
The reduction stage of the Calvin cycle involves the utilization of ATP and NADPH for the formation of carbohydrates.
Regeneration of the CO acceptor ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate is crucial for the Calvin cycle.
C3 plants have specific characteristics such as Kranz anatomy, tolerance to high temperatures, and absence of photorespiration.
The Hatch and Slack Pathway is a cyclic process that uses PEP carboxylase and phosphoenol pyruvate as the primary CO acceptor.
Hatch and Slack Pathway is the process by which CO2 is fixed into organic molecules in C3 plants, forming 4-carbon compounds like malic acid and aspartic acid.
Calvin pathway in C plants occurs in mesophyll cells and results in the formation of sugars and ATP.
Calvin pathway in C plants occurs in bundle sheath cells and is common to both C and C plants.
Photorespiration is a process that occurs in C plants, resulting in the release of CO and the utilization of ATP, without the synthesis of sugars and ATP.
RuBisCO enzyme can bind to both CO2 and O2, leading to competitive binding and the release of phosphoglycolate and phosphoglycerate in photorespiration.
Comparison of C plants and C plants based on the cell type where the Calvin cycle takes place, the initial carboxylation reaction occurs, and the number of cell types that fix CO.

CO fixation is the process of converting CO into organic forms, and PGA (3-carbon compound), OAA (4-carbon compound), RuBP (5-carbon compound), and PEP (4-carbon compound) are the possible primary fixation products.

concepts.concept_title: Number of Carbons in Primary CO Fixation Product
concepts.description: The primary CO fixation product can be a 3, 4, or 5-carbon compound.

concepts.concept_title: Presence of RuBisCO and PEP Case in Plants
concepts.description: Plants have RuBisCO (RuBP carboxylase/oxygenase) and PEP case enzymes for CO fixation.

concepts.concept_title: Cell Types in Plants Involved in CO Fixation
concepts.description: In plants, CO fixation occurs in mesophyll cells and bundle sheath cells.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Light Intensity on CO Fixation
concepts.description: CO fixation rates increase with increasing light intensity, but beyond a certain point, further increase in light intensity does not lead to a corresponding increase in CO fixation rates.
Blackman's Law of Limiting Factors states that the rate of photosynthesis is determined by the factor that is nearest to its minimal value.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of CO Concentration on Photosynthesis
concepts.description: The concentration of CO is the major limiting factor for photosynthesis, and an increase in CO concentration up to 0.05% can cause an increase in CO fixation rates.

concepts.concept_title: Light intensity and photosynthesis in C plants
concepts.description: C plants show saturation at about 360 µlL-1 of CO concentration, while C plants respond to higher CO levels and saturation is seen only beyond 450 µlL-1.

concepts.concept_title: Carbon dioxide fixation in C and C plants
concepts.description: C plants use the C pathway for carbon fixation, producing a 4-carbon compound in the mesophyll cells, whereas C plants use the Calvin cycle for carbon fixation.
The dark reactions are temperature-controlled, and C plants respond better to higher temperatures than C plants, which have a lower temperature optimum for photosynthesis.

concepts.concept_title: Water as a factor in photosynthesis
concepts.description: Water stress reduces CO availability and affects stomatal density and closure, leading to reduced photosynthesis.

concepts.concept_title: Mechanism of photosynthesis in C plants
concepts.description: Photosynthesis involves two stages: the light reaction and the carbon fixation cycle, involving the Calvin cycle and RuBisCO enzyme.

concepts.concept_title: Structure of thylakoids and light harvesting complexes
concepts.description: Thylakoids have a proton gradient that drives the synthesis of ATP, and light-harvesting complexes involve light-absorbing pigments such as chlorophyll a and P680.

concepts.concept_title: Light and Productivity in C Plants
concepts.description: C plants carry out the biosynthetic - Calvin pathway despite being highly productive, unlike C plants.

concepts.concept_title: RuBisCO and Carboxylation in C Plants
concepts.description: RuBisCO carries out more carboxylation in C plants due to its role as a carboxylase.
Chlorophyll b and other accessory pigments are present in plants to complement chlorophyll a in photosynthesis.
Chlorophyll a is more stable than chlorophyll b in dark conditions, which is why leaves often turn yellow or pale green.
Leaves exposed to sunlight are greener and have a higher concentration of chlorophyll a than shaded leaves.
There are different limiting factors at different points (A, B or C) of the photosynthesis curve.
The graph represents the effect of light on the rate of photosynthesis and shows different points (C and D) representing different conditions.
C pathway produces glucose from CO2, while C pathway produces ATP and NADPH.
Cyclic photophosphorylation generates ATP for C pathway, while non-cyclic photophosphorylation generates ATP for C pathway.

concepts.concept_title: Leaf Anatomy C and C Plants
concepts.description: C plants have a larger surface area to volume ratio, allowing them to carry out photosynthesis more efficiently.
Living organisms require energy for daily life activities, including absorption, transport, movement, reproduction, and breathing. Energy is obtained from food through cellular respiration.
Glycolysis is a series of chemical reactions that break down glucose into pyruvate, releasing energy in the form of ATP and NADH.
Fermentation is an anaerobic process that converts pyruvate into various products, such as lactic acid, ethanol, and CO2, releasing energy in the form of ATP.
Aerobic respiration is a process that converts pyruvate into ATP, using oxygen as a final electron acceptor, and releasing energy in the form of ATP, NADH, and FADH2.
The respiratory balance sheet represents the energy budget of an organism, showing the net energy gain or loss from the breakdown of carbohydrates, fats, and proteins.
Amphibolic pathways refer to metabolic processes that can be catabolic or anabolic, depending on the conditions, such as glycolysis and gluconeogenesis.
The respiratory quotient (RQ) is a measure of the efficiency of cellular respiration, calculated as the ratio of CO2 produced to O2 consumed.
Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants and cyanobacteria convert light energy into chemical energy, producing glucose and releasing oxygen.
Respiration in plants occurs in the cytoplasm and mitochondria, breaking down complex molecules to release energy, and producing carbon dioxide and water as byproducts.
Respiratory substrates are the compounds that are oxidized during cellular respiration, such as carbohydrates, proteins, and fats.
ATP serves as the energy currency of the cell, trapping energy from cellular respiration and releasing it as needed to fuel various cellular processes.
Plants require oxygen for respiration, which is obtained from the atmosphere and used to facilitate the breakdown of food molecules.
Plants have stomata and lenticels for gaseous exchange, replacing the need for respiratory organs.

concepts.concept_title: Plant Respiratory Rate is Lower than Animals
concepts.description: Plants respire at lower rates than animals, resulting in reduced gas exchange demands.
Living cells in plants have sufficient oxygen for oxygen-dependent processes due to their close proximity to the plant surface.
The complete combustion of glucose produces carbon dioxide, water, and energy as end products.
During respiration, energy from glucose is coupled to ATP synthesis through multi-step oxidation processes.
Organisms adapted to anaerobic conditions have enzymatic machinery for glucose breakdown without oxygen.
Glycolysis is the only process in respiration for anaerobic organisms, occurring in the cytoplasm and producing pyruvic acid.
In plants, glucose is derived from sucrose or storage carbohydrates, and undergoes phosphorylation to glucose-6-phosphate before entering the glycolytic pathway.
Phosphorylation of glucose is the process of converting glucose into glucose-6-phosphate, which is facilitated by the enzyme hexokinase.
Isomerisation of glucose-6-phosphate results in the formation of fructose-6-phosphate.
Glycolysis is a series of 10 reactions that lead to the conversion of glucose to pyruvate, with the involvement of various enzymes.
ATP is utilised at two stages in glycolysis: one during the conversion of glucose to glucose-6-phosphate and the other during the conversion of fructose-6-phosphate to fructose-1,6-bisphosphate.
During glycolysis, NAD+ is converted to NADH + H+, resulting in the removal of 2 electrons from PGAL, which produces one molecule of NADH.
Pyruvate can be metabolised through three different processes: aerobic respiration, lactic acid fermentation, and alcoholic fermentation.
In lactic acid fermentation, pyruvate is converted to lactic acid by the enzyme lactate dehydrogenase, with NADH + H+ being the reducing agent.
In alcoholic fermentation, pyruvate is converted to ethanol by the enzymes pyruvate decarboxylase and alcohol dehydrogenase.
During fermentation, less than 7% of the energy from glucose is released, resulting in the synthesis of less than 2 ATP molecules.
The process by which organisms can carry out complete oxidation of glucose and extract the energy stored to synthesise a larger number of ATP molecules.
The process that leads to a complete oxidation of organic substances in the presence of oxygen, and releases CO2, water and a large amount of energy.

concepts.concept_title: Role of mitochondria in aerobic respiration
concepts.description: The final product of glycolysis, pyruvate is transported from the cytoplasm into the mitochondria for aerobic respiration to take place.
The conversion of pyruvic acid to acetyl CoA in the presence of pyruvic dehydrogenase, NAD+ and Coenzyme A.
A cyclic pathway for the complete oxidation of acetyl CoA to CO2, producing ATP, NADH and FADH2.
The condensation of acetyl group with oxaloacetic acid to yield citric acid, followed by decarboxylation and oxidation reactions.
The synthesis of succinyl-CoA from succinic acid, releasing a molecule of CoA and producing a molecule of GTP.

concepts.concept_title: Role of NAD+ and FAD+ in TCA cycle
concepts.description: The reduction of NAD+ and FAD+ to NADH and FADH2 during the oxidation of acetyl CoA in the TCA cycle.
The summary equation for the TCA cycle is Pyruvic acid + 4NAD+ + FAD+ + 2H2O + ADP + Pi → 3 CO2 + 4NADH + 4H+ + 2FADH + ATP.

concepts.concept_title: Role of NADH and FADH in Respiration
concepts.description: NADH and FADH are synthesised during the TCA cycle and their oxidisation releases energy, which is used to synthesise ATP.
The ETS is a series of electron carriers in the inner mitochondrial membrane that transfer electrons from NADH and FADH to oxygen, driving the synthesis of ATP.

concepts.concept_title: Number of ATP Molecules from NADH and FADH
concepts.description: One molecule of NADH gives rise to 3 molecules of ATP, while one molecule of FADH produces 2 molecules of ATP.
Oxygen acts as the final hydrogen acceptor in the electron transport system, driving the synthesis of ATP.
Oxidative phosphorylation is the process by which the energy released during the electron transport system is used to synthesise ATP.
Complexes I, II, III, IV, and V of the electron transport system are responsible for transferring electrons and synthesising ATP.

concepts.concept_title: Mechanism of ATP Synthesis in Electron Transport System
concepts.description: The energy released during the electron transport system is utilised to synthesise ATP with the help of ATP synthase, driven by the proton gradient.
The electrochemical proton gradient is a key component of the electron transport system that drives ATP synthesis in mitochondria.
F1 ATP synthetase is a protein complex that forms the channel through which protons cross the inner mitochondrial membrane to produce ATP.
The electron transport system is a series of protein complexes located in the inner mitochondrial membrane that generate a proton gradient to drive ATP synthesis.
Aerobic respiration produces a net gain of 38 ATP molecules from the breakdown of one glucose molecule.
Fermentation is a partial breakdown of glucose that produces only a net gain of 2 ATP molecules.
The respiratory pathway can operate both as a catabolic process to break down substrates and as an anabolic process to synthesize them.
The respiratory pathway is a combination of anabolism and catabolism, allowing for the breakdown and synthesis of molecules.
The ratio of CO2 volume evolved to O2 volume consumed during respiration, which depends on the type of respiratory substrate.
The process of breaking down complex organic molecules through oxidation, releasing energy in the form of ATP.
The initial stage of cellular respiration, where one glucose molecule is broken down into two pyruvic acid molecules through a series of enzyme-catalysed reactions.
A series of enzyme-catalysed reactions occurring in the mitochondria, where pyruvic acid is converted into acetyl CoA and energy is generated in the form of NADH and FADH.
A system of electron carriers located on the inner membrane of the mitochondria, which helps to generate ATP through oxidative phosphorylation.
The process of generating ATP through the electron transport system, where oxygen is the ultimate acceptor of electrons and gets reduced to water.
Organic molecules produced during photosynthesis, breakdown of proteins, or direct consumption by animals serve as respiratory substrates.
A multi-step process converting glucose into pyruvate with a net gain of 2 ATP and 2 NADH.
Aerobic respiration includes glycolysis, Krebs cycle, and electron transport chain; taking place in mitochondria.
A multi-step process converting glucose into pyruvate with a net gain of 2 ATP and 2 NADH.
A series of electron carriers in the mitochondrial inner membrane, driving the synthesis of ATP.
Aerobic respiration uses O2, produces NADH and FADH2, whereas anaerobic respiration lacks O2 and NADH and FADH2.

concepts.concept_title: Net gain of ATP calculation assumptions
concepts.description: Assumes no energy loss or gain during glycolysis, Krebs cycle, and electron transport chain and neglects H^+ ions.
The respiratory pathway uses intermediates for both catabolism and anabolism.

concepts.concept_title: RQ and its value for fats
concepts.description: RQ is the ratio of CO2 produced to O2 consumed during respiration, with a value of 0.7 for fats.
The synthesis of ATP driven by the transfer of electrons in the electron transport chain.

concepts.concept_title: Step-wise release of energy in respiration
concepts.description: Energy in food is released as ATP, NADH, and FADH2 through step-wise process of oxidation.
Growth is an irreversible permanent increase in size of an organ or its parts or even of an individual cell.
Plant growth is unique because plants retain the capacity for unlimited growth throughout their life due to the presence of meristems.
Cells in meristems have the capacity to divide and self-perpetuate, leading to addition of new cells to the plant body.
Root apical meristem and shoot apical meristem are responsible for primary growth, while lateral meristems like vascular cambium and cork-cambium contribute to secondary growth.
Development is the sum of two processes: growth and differentiation.
Seed germination is the first step in the process of plant growth, which occurs when favourable conditions exist in the environment.
Factors that govern and control development are both intrinsic (internal) and extrinsic (external) to the plant.

concepts.concept_title: Increase in Protoplasm Leads to Growth
concepts.description: Growth is largely due to an increase in the amount of protoplasm. Since measuring protoplasm increase is difficult, growth is measured through other parameters.
Growth can be measured by various parameters including increase in fresh weight, dry weight, length, area, volume, and cell number.
The growth of a plant can be divided into three phases: meristematic, elongation, and maturation. Each phase represents a different stage of cell division and expansion.
The meristematic phase is characterized by rapidly dividing cells, large nuclei, and primary cell walls. This phase is present at the root apex and shoot apex.
The elongation phase is characterized by increased vacuolation, cell enlargement, and new cell wall deposition. This phase occurs immediately behind the meristematic region.
The maturation phase is characterized by maximal cell size, wall thickening, and protoplasmic modifications. This phase occurs further away from the apex.
The growth rate is the increase in growth per unit time and can be expressed mathematically. It shows an increase that may be arithmetic or geometrical.
Arithmetic growth is characterized by a constant linear increase in size, where only one daughter cell continues to divide.
Geometric growth is characterized by an exponential increase in size, where both progeny cells retain the ability to divide.
A sigmoid curve is a characteristic of living organism growing in a natural environment, typical for all cells, tissues, and organs of a plant.
The exponential growth can be expressed as W = W0e^(rt), where W is the final size, W0 is the initial size, r is the growth rate, t is the time, and e is the base of natural logarithms.

Absolute growth rate is the measurement and comparison of total growth per unit time, while relative growth rate is the growth of the given system per unit time expressed on a common basis, such as per unit initial parameter.
Plant growth requires water, oxygen, nutrients, and optimal temperature range. Environmental signals such as light and gravity also affect certain phases/stages of growth.
Differentiation is the process of cells undergoing structural changes to perform specific functions, resulting in maturation and loss of ability to divide.
Dedifferentiation is the phenomenon where differentiated cells regain the capacity to divide under certain conditions, such as formation of meristems from fully differentiated parenchyma cells.
Redifferentiation is the process where dedifferentiated cells regain their functional characteristics, resulting in re-formation of specialized cells and tissues.

concepts.concept_title: Open vs. Determinate Growth in Plants
concepts.description: Growth in plants is considered open or indeterminate when it can continue indefinitely, and determinate when it has a fixed endpoint.
Tissues in woody dicotyledonous plants undergo redifferentiation, where they mature and perform specific functions, such as cell specialization.
Differentiation is a process where undifferentiated cells become specialized in structure and function, resulting in different types of tissues and organs.
Cells/tissues in plants exhibit open differentiation, where the position of a cell or tissue determines its final structure at maturity.

concepts.concept_title: Plant Tissue Culture: Tumour vs. Callus
concepts.description: Tumours in plant tissue culture are masses of undifferentiated cells that exhibit abnormal growth, while callus is a mass of differentiated cells that have undergone redifferentiation.
Plasticity refers to the ability of plants to develop different shapes, forms, and structures in response to environmental stimuli or developmental stages.
Development in plants encompasses all changes that occur during the life cycle from germination to senescence, including growth, differentiation, and maturation.

concepts.concept_title: Intrinsic and Extrinsic Factors Affecting Plant Development
concepts.description: Plant development is under the control of intrinsic (genetic and chemical) and extrinsic (environmental) factors.
PGRs are small, simple molecules that regulate plant growth and development by promoting growth (e.g., cell division, elongation) or inhibiting growth (e.g., dormancy).

concepts.concept_title: Classification of PGRs Based on Function
concepts.description: PGRs can be broadly classified into two groups: growth-promoting (e.g., auxins, gibberellins, cytokinins) and growth-inhibiting (e.g., abscisic acid) PGRs.
Gibberellic acid (GA) is a type of gibberellin that causes an increase in axis length, delays senescence, and promotes fruit elongation. It is used in various agricultural practices.
Gibberellins produce a wide range of physiological responses in plants, including increased axis length, fruit elongation, and delayed senescence.
Gibberellins are used to increase plant height, improve fruit shape, and speed up the malting process in the brewing industry.
Cytokinins are plant growth regulators that promote cell division, produce new leaves, and stimulate lateral shoot growth.
Cytokinins help overcome apical dominance and promote nutrient mobilization, leading to delayed leaf senescence.

concepts.concept_title: Effects of Ethylene on Plant Growth
concepts.description: Ethylene promotes horizontal seedling growth, apical hook formation, and abscission of plant organs.

concepts.concept_title: Role of Ethylene in Fruit Ripening
concepts.description: Ethylene promotes fruit ripening, enhances respiration rate, and induces flowering and fruit-set in certain plants.
Ethylene is used to initiate flowering, synchronise fruit-set, and induce flowering in mango and pineapples.

concepts.concept_title: Ethephon as a Source of Ethylene
concepts.description: Ethephon is a widely used compound that releases ethylene slowly and promotes fruit ripening and abscission.

concepts.concept_title: Role of ABA in Plant Growth and Development
concepts.description: ABA acts as a general plant growth inhibitor and a stress hormone by regulating abscission, dormancy, seed germination, and stomatal closure.

concepts.concept_title: Antagonistic Role of ABA and GAs
concepts.description: ABA acts as an antagonist to gibberellins (GAs) in various phases of growth, differentiation, and development.
PGRs are chemical substances that control differentiation and developmental events in plants, and are classified into five groups: auxins, gibberellins, cytokinins, abscisic acid, and ethylene.

concepts.concept_title: Synergistic and Antagonistic Effects of PGRs
concepts.description: PGRs can act synergistically or antagonistically to exert their physiological effects on plants.

concepts.concept_title: Intrinsic and Extrinsic Factors Controlling Plant Growth and Development
concepts.description: Plant growth and development are under the control of both intrinsic (PGRs) and extrinsic factors (light, temperature, nutrition, oxygen status, gravity).
Plant growth can be divided into three phases: lag, log, and senescent phase.
Differentiation is the process by which a cell loses the capacity to divide, resulting in the development of structures commensurate with the function of the cell.
Plant development can be flexible, as evidenced by the ability of plants to exhibit plasticity in development.
Determinate growth is a type of growth that has a fixed endpoint and a limited growth rate.
Arithmetic growth is a type of growth that occurs when growth rate is constant over time.
Geometric growth is a type of growth that occurs when growth rate increases exponentially over time.
A sigmoid growth curve is a graph that shows the slow growth, rapid growth, and slow decline in growth rate of a species.
Relative growth rate is a measure of growth rate that compares the change in size over time to the starting size.
Abscisic acid is a plant hormone that responds to stress and promotes plant survival during abiotic stresses.
Plant growth regulators are a group of substances that regulate growth and development of plants.
Cytokinin is used to induce rooting in cuttings, delay leaf senescence, and promote plant growth.
A defoliated plant is not responsive to photoperiodic cycle due to loss of leaf tissues.
GA application in rice plants results in elongation of stem and suppression of seedlings.
Cytokinin is essential for cell division and preventing differentiation in cell cultures.

concepts.concept_title: Reductionist approach to study of life forms
concepts.description: The reductionist approach reduces complex biological processes to simpler physico-chemical concepts and techniques.
Molecular biology is the study of living organisms in terms of their molecular components and interactions.
Systems biology is the study of living organisms as systems, considering interactions among components, to understand emergent properties.
Simple diffusion over the entire body surface or through cuticle, tracheal tubes, gills, or lungs allows gas exchange in invertebrates and vertebrates.
The human respiratory system consists of a pair of lungs and a system of airways (trachea, bronchi, etc.) for gas exchange.
Breathing involves the exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide between the atmosphere and the blood, facilitated by the respiratory organs.
The regulation of breathing includes the control of rate and depth of breathing, involving the respiratory centers in the brain and the respiratory organs.
Disorders of the respiratory system include conditions affecting the lungs, airways, and respiratory organs, such as respiratory failure and lung diseases.

The respiratory system consists of the nostrils, nasal passage, nasal chamber, pharynx, larynx, trachea, bronchi, bronchioles, and lungs. It has two main parts: the conducting part and the respiratory or exchange part.
The epiglottis is a thin elastic cartilaginous flap that covers the glottis to prevent the entry of food into the larynx during swallowing.
The trachea is a straight tube that extends up to the mid-thoracic cavity, supported by incomplete cartilaginous rings.
The trachea divides into primary bronchi, which in turn divide into secondary and tertiary bronchi, and then into bronchioles, ending in terminal bronchioles.
Alveoli are thin, irregular-walled, and vascularised bag-like structures that give rise to the respiratory part of the respiratory system.
The lungs are covered by a double-layered pleura, with pleural fluid between them, which reduces friction on the lung surface.

concepts.concept_title: Conducting and Respiratory Parts of the Respiratory System
concepts.description: The conducting part transports atmospheric air to the alveoli, while the respiratory part is the site of actual diffusion of gases between blood and atmospheric air.
Breathing involves two stages: inspiration and expiration, carried out by creating a pressure gradient between the lungs and the atmosphere.

concepts.concept_title: Role of the Diaphragm in Breathing
concepts.description: The diaphragm contracts to initiate inspiration, creating a negative pressure in the lungs with respect to atmospheric pressure.
Breathing involves the increase and decrease in thoracic volume due to the contraction and relaxation of external inter-costal muscles and diaphragm.
There are three types of breathing movements: normal breathing, forced inspiration and forced expiration.

concepts.concept_title: Volume of Air Involved in Breathing
concepts.description: The volume of air involved in breathing is approximately 500 mL and can be estimated using a spirometer.
Respiratory volumes include tidal volume, inspiratory reserve volume, expiratory reserve volume and residual volume.
Pulmonary capacities include inspiratory capacity, expiratory capacity, functional residual capacity, vital capacity and total lung capacity.
Exchange of gases occur between alveoli and blood or tissues due to simple diffusion based on pressure/concentration gradient.
Partial pressure of a gas is the pressure contributed by an individual gas in a mixture of gases.

concepts.concept_title: Partial pressures of gases in atmospheric air and alveoli
concepts.description: Partial pressures of oxygen and carbon dioxide in atmospheric air and alveoli are crucial for gas exchange. The partial pressures of these gases in different sites like blood and tissues also play a significant role.
A concentration gradient is present for oxygen from alveoli to blood and tissues, whereas for carbon dioxide, it is from tissues to blood and alveoli.

concepts.concept_title: Diffusion of gases through the alveolar-capillary membrane
concepts.description: The diffusion membrane is made up of three layers, and its thickness is much less than a millimeter. The solubility of carbon dioxide is 20-25 times higher than that of oxygen.
About 97% of oxygen is transported by RBCs in the blood, and the remaining 3% is carried in a dissolved state through the plasma.
Haemoglobin is a red-colored iron-containing pigment present in RBCs, and oxygen can bind to it in a reversible manner to form oxyhaemoglobin.

The oxygen dissociation curve is a sigmoid curve showing the percentage saturation of haemoglobin with oxygen against partial pressure of oxygen, and is useful in studying the effect of factors like partial pressure of carbon dioxide, hydrogen ion concentration, etc., on oxygen binding.

concepts.concept_title: Transport of carbon dioxide in blood
concepts.description: Carbon dioxide is carried by haemoglobin as carbamino-haemoglobin (about 20-25%) and in a dissolved state through the plasma, and about 70% is carried as bicarbonate.
Diffusion of gases occurs in the alveolar region by the process of diffusion, which is dependent on partial pressure gradients and solubility.
Oxygen is transported mainly as oxyhaemoglobin, bound to haemoglobin in the alveoli and dissociated at the tissues.
Carbon dioxide is transported as bicarbonate (70-80%) and carbamino-haemoglobin (20-25%), with the help of the enzyme carbonic anhydrase.

concepts.concept_title: Partial Pressure of Gases in Atmosphere and Alveoli
concepts.description: The partial pressure of oxygen (pO) is higher in alveoli than in the atmospheric air, while the partial pressure of carbon dioxide (pCO) is lower in alveoli.
Inspiration occurs due to the contraction of intercostal muscles and the diaphragm, which increases the volume of air in the lungs.
Respiration is regulated by the respiratory centre in the medulla, pneumotaxic centre in the pons, and chemosensitive area in the medulla.
The oxygen dissociation curve shows the relationship between the partial pressure of oxygen and the saturation of haemoglobin with oxygen, with a sigmoidal pattern.
Hypoxia is a condition characterized by insufficient oxygen supply to the tissues, which can occur in various situations.

concepts.concept_title: Vital Capacity and Total Lung Capacity
concepts.description: Vital capacity is the maximum volume of air that can be expelled from the lungs after maximum inhalation, while total lung capacity is the sum of vital capacity, residual volume, and expiratory reserve volume.
Tidal volume is the volume of air inspired or expired during normal breathing, which is approximately 500 ml for a healthy human in an hour.
Blood is a special connective tissue consisting of a fluid matrix, plasma, and formed elements.
Plasma is a straw-colored, viscous fluid containing nearly 55% of blood, with 90-92% being water and proteins contributing 6-8%.
Fibrinogen is needed for clotting or coagulation of blood.
Globulins primarily involved in defense mechanisms of the body.
Albumins help in osmotic balance.
Leucocytes are nucleated and relatively fewer in number, averaging 6000-8000 mm-3 of blood.
There are two main categories of leucocytes - granulocytes and agranulocytes.
Neutrophils and monocytes are phagocytic cells that destroy foreign organisms entering the body.
Basophils secrete histamine, serotonin, heparin, etc., and are involved in inflammatory reactions.
Eosinophils resist infections and are also involved in inflammatory reactions.
RBCs have an average lifespan of 120 days.
RBCs are destroyed in the spleen after their lifespan is over.

Blood is composed of formed elements like erythrocytes, leukocytes, and thrombocytes. Erythrocytes (RBCs) are responsible for oxygen transport, leukocytes (including B and T lymphocytes) are involved in immune responses, and thrombocytes (platelets) are involved in blood clotting.
Platelets are cell fragments produced from megakaryocytes in the bone marrow. They release substances involved in blood clotting and are essential for preventing excessive blood loss.

ABO blood grouping is based on the presence or absence of A and B surface antigens on RBCs. Plasma contains natural antibodies against A and B antigens, and blood grouping is crucial for transfusions to prevent immune responses.

Donor compatibility is essential for transfusions, with certain blood groups (A, B, AB, O) being compatible or incompatible based on ABO blood grouping and Rh factor. Universal donors and universal recipients are identified based on their blood group.

Rh antigen is present on RBCs of majority of humans and is crucial for transfusions. Rh-ve individuals can form antibodies against Rh+ve blood, leading to erythroblastosis foetalis in cases of mismatching.
Blood coagulation is essential for preventing excessive bleeding and is influenced by platelets, clotting factors, and other components. The coagulation cascade involves various steps and pathways.
Blood coagulation occurs in response to an injury, forming a solid blood clot to prevent excessive blood loss.
Platelets in the blood release factors that activate the coagulation mechanism in response to an injury.
Calcium ions play a crucial role in the coagulation of blood.
Blood plasma contains various components, including fibrinogens, prothrombin, and thrombin.
Lymph is formed from interstitial fluid from the cells of tissues, collected by the lymphatic system.
Lymph carries specialized lymphocytes, nutrients, hormones, and fats.
Circulatory systems can be open or closed, with open systems allowing fluid to flow into open spaces, while closed systems regulate fluid flow through a network of vessels.

concepts.concept_title: Types of Hearts and Circulatory Pathways
concepts.description: Different organisms have different types of hearts and circulatory pathways, including single circulation, incomplete double circulation, and complete double circulation.
The human circulatory system consists of a network of blood vessels and a muscular chambered heart that pumps blood throughout the body.

The human heart is a muscular, chambered organ located in the thoracic cavity, protected by the pericardium. It consists of four chambers: two atria and two ventricles, separated by septa. The atria receive blood from the veins, and the ventricles pump blood into the pulmonary artery and aorta, respectively.

The heart is composed of cardiac muscles, with the ventricles having thicker walls than the atria. The cardiac musculature is specialized, with nodal tissue generating action potentials without external stimuli. The sino-atrial node is the pacemaker of the heart, responsible for initiating and maintaining the rhythmic contractile activity.

The cardiac cycle involves four stages: diastole of the four chambers, atrial systole, ventricular systole, and diastole. The sino-atrial node generates an action potential, stimulating both atria to contract. This increases blood flow into the ventricles, which then contract, pumping blood into the pulmonary artery and aorta.

The heart has valves that allow blood to flow in one direction: from the atria to the ventricles, and from the ventricles to the pulmonary artery and aorta. The tricuspid valve and mitral valve are located between the atria and ventricles, while the semilunar valves are located between the ventricles and the pulmonary artery and aorta.

Ventricular systole increases ventricular pressure, closing tricuspid and bicuspid valves, whereas ventricular diastole causes closure of semilunar valves, allowing blood to flow back into the ventricles.
The cardiac cycle consists of systole and diastole of both atria and ventricles, repeated cyclically to maintain continuous blood circulation.
Stroke volume is the volume of blood pumped out by each ventricle per beat, and cardiac output is the volume of blood pumped out by each ventricle per minute.
An ECG is a graphical representation of the electrical activity of the heart during a cardiac cycle, represented by waveforms P, Q, R, S, and T.
The first and second heart sounds are produced by the closure of tricuspid and bicuspid valves and semilunar valves, respectively, and are of clinical diagnostic significance.
Counting QRS complexes in a given time period helps determine the heartbeat rate of an individual.
Any deviation in ECG shape indicates a possible abnormality or disease.
