The blood flows through a fixed route in blood vessels (arтерies and veins), consisting of three layers: tunica intima, tunica media, and tunica externa.
Pulmonary circulation involves oxygenated blood returning to the lungs, while systemic circulation involves deoxygenated blood being carried to the tissues.
A vascular connection exists between the digestive tract and liver, enabling the hepatic portal vein to carry blood from the intestine to the liver before delivery to the systemic circulation.
The heart's normal activities are regulated intrinsically by myocardial muscles and can be moderated by the autonomic nervous system.
The cardiac output can be increased by sympathetic neural signals and adrenal medullary hormones, while parasympathetic neural signals decrease it.
Hypertension is characterized by blood pressure higher than normal (120/80 mm Hg), leading to heart diseases and affecting vital organs.
CAD, or atherosclerosis, affects the vessels supplying blood to the heart muscle due to calcium, fat, cholesterol, and fibrous tissue deposits.
Angina, or 'angina pectoris', is a symptom of acute chest pain caused by insufficient oxygen reaching the heart muscle, often occurring in middle-aged and elderly individuals.
Heart failure occurs when the heart is unable to pump blood effectively to meet the body's needs, resulting in congestion in the lungs.
Blood comprises a fluid matrix (plasma) and formed elements (RBCs, WBCs, and platelets) that transport essential substances to cells and carry waste products away.
Blood is grouped into A, B, AB, and O systems based on the presence or absence of surface antigens A and B, and Rh factor, which determines compatibility for blood transfusions.
Tissue fluid, also known as lymph, is a fluid derived from blood that transports substances between cells and tissues.
A closed circulatory system, found in vertebrates and some invertebrates, uses a network of vessels and a fluid (blood) to transport substances throughout the body.
The heart is a muscular pumping organ with two atria and two ventricles, responsible for pumping blood throughout the body through a network of vessels.
Cardiac musculature is auto-excitable, allowing the heart to generate electrical impulses independently, which are crucial for regulating heartbeats.
The sino-atrial node, or pacemaker, generates electrical impulses at a rate of 70-75 beats per minute, regulating the heartbeat.
The cardiac cycle, comprising systole and diastole, consists of the heart pumping blood into the pulmonary artery and aorta, with 70 mL pumped out by each ventricle per beat.
The cardiac output, or volume of blood pumped by the heart per minute, is the product of stroke volume and heart rate, approximating 5 liters per minute.
The human circulatory system features double circulation, comprising pulmonary and systemic pathways, ensuring that oxygenated and deoxygenated blood are carried efficiently.

concepts.concept_title: Open and Closed System of Circulation
concepts.description: Open system of circulation is present in most of the lower vertebrates whereas in higher vertebrates, closed system of circulation is present.
Systole is the period of contraction of heart, whereas diastole is the period of relaxation.
P-wave represents the contraction of atria whereas T-wave represents the contraction of ventricles.
The pattern of heart in vertebrates undergoes a change from simple fish-like heart to complex vertebrate heart, having four-chambered structure.
Our heart is called myogenic as it develops from the mesodermic layer of embryo, which is capable of undergoing spontaneous contraction.
The sino-atrial node acts as the pacemaker of the heart as it generates and conducts impulse at a rate of 70-100 beats per minute.
Atrio-ventricular node and atrio-ventricular bundle play crucial roles in the functioning of heart by delaying and conducting impulses.
A cardiac cycle consists of diastole, rapid ventricular contraction, rapid ventricular relaxation, slow ventricular contraction and complete filling of ventricles.
Cardiac output is the volume of blood pumped by the heart in one minute.
Heart sounds are produced due to the closure of semilunar and atrioventricular valves during systolic and diastolic phases of cardiac cycle.
A standard ECG has six segments, namely P-wave, QRS complex, T-wave, P-R interval, O-T interval and T-P interval, representing different stages of cardiac cycle.
Humans have to eliminate various substances like ammonia, urea, uric acid, carbon dioxide, and ions accumulated by metabolic activities or excess ingestion.
Urine is formed in the kidneys through filtration and concentration of the filtrate.
Animals excrete ammonia, urea, and uric acid as nitrogenous wastes with varying amounts of water loss depending on their ecological adaptations.
Ammonia can be eliminated by diffusion across body surfaces or through gill surfaces in fish, with little role played by the kidneys.
Mammals, terrestrial amphibians, and marine fishes excrete urea as a lesser toxic nitrogenous waste, conserving water and requiring the liver to convert ammonia to urea.
Birds, reptiles, land snails, and insects excrete nitrogenous wastes as uric acid with minimal water loss, called uricotelism.

Protonephridia or flame cells are excretory structures found in invertebrates like flatworms, rotifers, some annelids, and cephalochordates, primarily concerned with ionic and fluid volume regulation (osmoregulation).
Nephridia are the tubular excretory structures found in earthworms and other annelids, helping in the removal of nitrogenous wastes and maintaining fluid and ionic balance.
Malpighian tubules are excretory structures found in most insects, including cockroaches, helping in the removal of nitrogenous wastes and osmoregulation.
The human excretory system consists of a pair of kidneys, ureters, urinary bladder, and urethra.
Each kidney consists of an outer cortex and an inner medulla with the renal pelvis, calyces, and hilum.
A nephron is a functional unit of the kidney consisting of a glomerulus and a renal tubule.
The glomerulus consists of afferent and efferent arterioles and is surrounded by Bowman's capsule.

concepts.concept_title: Functions of the Proximal Convoluted Tubule (PCT)
concepts.description: The PCT is responsible for reabsorption of glucose, amino acids, and ions from the filtrate.
The renal tubule consists of the proximal convoluted tubule, Henle's loop, and the distal convoluted tubule (DCT).

concepts.concept_title: Functions of the Loop of Henle
concepts.description: The loop of Henle is responsible for concentration and dilution of the urine.
Nephrons are classified into cortical nephrons and juxta medullary nephrons based on the length of the loop of Henle.
Glomerular filtration is the process of ultrafiltration of blood through the glomerular capillary walls.
GFR is the rate at which filtrate is formed by the kidneys per minute.
Juxtaglomerular apparatus (JGA) is a sensitive region that regulates blood pressure by releasing renin in response to a fall in glomerular filtration rate (GFR).
Most of the filtrate is reabsorbed by the renal tubules, either by active or passive transport, to maintain ionic and acid-base balance of body fluids.

concepts.concept_title: Substances Reabsorbed by the Proximal Convoluted Tubule (PCT)
concepts.description: The PCT reabsorbs nearly all essential nutrients, 70-80% electrolytes, and water, and maintains pH and ionic balance of body fluids.
Reabsorption in Henle’s loop is minimal, but it plays a crucial role in maintaining high osmolarity of medullary interstitial fluid.

concepts.concept_title: Conditional Reabsorption by Distal Convoluted Tubule (DCT)
concepts.description: The DCT reabsorbs Na+ and water conditionally, and also reabsorbs HCO– and selectively secretes hydrogen and potassium ions.
The collecting duct allows the reabsorption of large amounts of water, and also plays a role in maintaining pH and ionic balance of blood.

concepts.concept_title: Counter Current Mechanism in Henle’s Loop and Vasa Recta
concepts.description: The counter current mechanism between Henle’s loop and vasa recta maintains an increasing osmolarity towards the inner medullary interstitium.
The transport of substances in the nephron facilitated by the special arrangement of Henle's loop and vasa recta, maintaining a concentration gradient in the medullary interstitium.

concepts.concept_title: Hormonal Feedback Mechanisms in Kidney Function
concepts.description: The hormonal mechanisms involving the hypothalamus, JGA, and heart that regulate kidney function, including the release of antidiuretic hormone (ADH) and renin.
A complex mechanism regulating kidney function, including the release of renin and angiotensin II, which increases glomerular blood pressure and GFR.
A hormone released by the heart that causes vasodilation and decreases blood pressure, acting as a check on the renin-angiotensin mechanism.
The neural mechanisms causing the release of urine, initiated by the stretching of the urinary bladder and involving the CNS and smooth muscles of the bladder.
The physical and chemical properties of urine, including its pH, colour, and odour, which can affect clinical diagnosis and kidney function.
The process of glucose reabsorption and excretion in the kidneys, which can be affected by various conditions, including diabetes.

concepts.concept_title: Role of Other Organs in Excretion
concepts.description: Other than the kidneys, lungs, liver, and skin help in the elimination of excretory wastes.
The liver secretes bile-containing substances like bilirubin, biliverdin, cholesterol, and degraded steroid hormones.
Sweat and sebaceous glands in the skin eliminate certain substances like NaCl, urea, and sterols through sweat and sebum.
Nitrogenous wastes like urea are present in urine, which can be used as an indicator of diabetes mellitus (ketonuria).

concepts.concept_title: Kidney Function in Regulation of Water
concepts.description: The kidneys help in the conservation of water along with CO2 elimination.
Malfunctioning of kidneys can lead to accumulation of urea in blood, a condition called uremia.

concepts.concept_title: Role of Dialysis in Removing Urea
concepts.description: Dialysis (haemodialysis) is a process used to remove urea and other nitrogenous wastes from the blood.

concepts.concept_title: Renal Transplantation as a Method to Treat Kidney Failure
concepts.description: Kidney transplantation is a method used to treat acute renal failures by replacing the non-functioning kidney with a healthy one from a donor.
Renal calculi are insoluble masses of crystallized salts formed in the kidneys.
Glomerulonephritis is an inflammation of the glomeruli of the kidney.
Nitrogenous wastes like ammonia, urea, and uric acid are eliminated through the excretory system.

concepts.concept_title: Excretory Organs Found in Various Animals
concepts.description: Animals have various types of excretory organs like protonephridia, nephridia, malpighian tubules, and green glands.

concepts.concept_title: Nephron as the Functional Unit of the Kidney
concepts.description: A nephron is the functional unit of the kidney and consists of the glomerulus and renal tubule.
A nephron has different parts like Bowman's capsule, proximal convoluted tubule, Henle's loop, and distal convoluted tubule.
GFR is the rate at which blood is filtered by the glomerulus to form filtrate in the Bowman's capsule per minute.
Filtration is a non-selective process performed by the glomerulus using glomerular capillary blood pressure to form filtrate in the Bowman's capsule.
About 99% reabsorption of the filtrate takes place through different parts of the nephrons, with PCT being the major site.
JGA plays a significant role in the regulation of GFR.
HL primarily helps to maintain osmolar gradient within the kidney interstitium.
Counter current mechanism operates between the two limbs of the loop of Henle and those of vasa recta to concentrate the filtrate.
Skin, lungs and liver also assist in excretion, besides the kidneys.
Osmoregulation is the process by which the body maintains osmolar gradient within the kidney interstitium.
Terrestrial animals are generally either ureotelic or uricotelic, not ammonotelic.
Uricotelism is the process by which animals excrete uric acid as their end product of nitrogen metabolism.
Animals and plants exhibit a wide range of movements, including streaming of protoplasm, movement of cilia, flagella, and tentacles.
Locomotion is a type of voluntary movement that results in a change of place or location.
Locomotion methods vary with habitats and situations, including search for food, shelter, mate, breeding grounds, or to escape from enemies/predators.
There are three types of muscles: skeletal, visceral, and cardiac, based on their location and properties.
Skeletal muscles are striated, voluntary muscles involved in locomotory actions and changes of body postures.
Muscles have properties like excitability, contractility, extensibility, and elasticity.
Muscles can be classified based on their location, appearance, and nature of regulation of their activities.

concepts.concept_title: Types of Muscles: Skeletal, Cardiac, and Smooth
concepts.description: Muscles in the human body are broadly classified into three types: skeletal, cardiac, and smooth, based on their structure and location.
Skeletal muscle is made up of muscle bundles or fascicles held together by a common collagenous connective tissue layer called fascia, with each fascicle containing a number of muscle fibres.
Each muscle fibre is lined by the plasma membrane called sarcolemma, enclosing the sarcoplasm, which contains many nuclei, and the sarcoplasmic reticulum, which is the store house of calcium ions.

The sarcoplasm of the muscle fibre contains a characteristic feature of parallelly arranged filaments in the sarcoplasm called myofilaments or myofibrils, each consisting of actin and myosin proteins.
The myofibrils have alternate light and dark bands on them, with the light bands containing actin and called I-bands, and the dark bands containing myosin and called A-bands.
The portion of the myofibril between two successive Z-lines is called a sarcomere, considered as the functional unit of contraction.
Each actin filament is made of two 'F' actins helically wound to each other, each 'F' actin being a polymer of monomeric 'G' globular actins.
Tropomyosin and troponin proteins are distributed on the actin filaments, with troponin masking the active binding sites for myosin on the actin filaments in the resting state.
Each myosin filament is a polymerised protein, made up of many monomeric proteins called myosin heads.
Meromyosins constitute one thick filament and have two important parts, heavy meromyosin (HMM) and light meromyosin (LMM).
The globular head (or heavy meromyosin) is an ATPase enzyme with binding sites for ATP and actin, and has a short arm and a tail.

concepts.concept_title: Sliding Filament Theory of Muscle Contraction
concepts.description: Muscle contraction occurs by the sliding of thin filaments over thick filaments in a muscle fibre.

concepts.concept_title: Role of Calcium in Muscle Contraction
concepts.description: Calcium ions bind with troponin and cause the unmasking of active sites on actin filaments, allowing myosin heads to bind to them.
The myosin head binds to the exposed active sites on actin to form a cross bridge, which pulls the attached actin filaments towards the centre of the A band.
Muscle relaxation occurs when calcium ions are pumped back to the sarcoplasmic cisternae, masking the actin filaments and causing the return of Z lines to their original position.
Red fibres have high myoglobin content, plenty of mitochondria, and utilise oxygen for ATP production, while white fibres have low myoglobin content and rely on anaerobic processes for energy.
The skeletal system consists of a system of bones, cartilages, and ligaments that provide support and movement to the body.
Bones have a hard matrix due to calcium salts and cartilage has a pliable matrix due to chondroitin salts.
The axial skeleton consists of 80 bones that form the main axis of the body and protects vital organs.
The axial skeleton is divided into two sections: the skull and the vertebral column.
The skull is composed of 22 bones, divided into cranial and facial bones, which protect the brain.
The ear ossicles (Malleus, Incus, and Stapes) transmit sound vibrations from the outer ear to the inner ear.
The vertebral column consists of 26 units called vertebrae, which protect the spinal cord and support the head.
The vertebral column is divided into cervical, thoracic, lumbar, sacral, and coccygeal regions.
The sternum serves as a point of attachment for the ribs and musculature of the back.
Ribs are classified into true ribs, vertebrochondral ribs, and floating ribs.
The appendicular skeleton consists of bones of the limbs and their girdles.
The limbs and their girdles are composed of specific bones that work together to provide movement and support.

concepts.concept_title: Articulation of Upper and Lower Limbs
concepts.description: Bones help in articulation of upper and lower limbs with the axial skeleton. Upper limb is articulated with scapula and clavicle, while lower limb is articulated with coxal bones.
Pectoral girdle consists of clavicle and scapula. Each scapula has a spine, acromion, and glenoid cavity. The clavicle articulates with the acromion.
Pelvic girdle consists of two coxal bones, formed by the fusion of ilium, ischium, and pubis. The two halves meet to form the pubic symphysis.
Joints are classified into three major structural forms: fibrous, cartilaginous, and synovial. Fibrous joints do not allow movement, while cartilaginous and synovial joints allow movement.
Synovial joints allow considerable movement, facilitating locomotion and other movements. Examples include ball and socket, hinge, pivot, gliding, and saddle joints.
Muscular disorders include myasthenia gravis, muscular dystrophy, tetany, arthritis, osteoporosis, and gout. These disorders affect the muscular and skeletal system.
Skeletal muscles are attached to skeletal elements, striated, and voluntary.
Visceral muscles are nonstriated and involuntary, while cardiac muscles are striated, branched, and involuntary.
Muscles possess excitability, contractility, extensibility, and elasticity.
A sarcomere has a central 'A' band made of thick myosin filaments and two half 'I' bands made of thin actin filaments.
Myosin and actin are polymerised proteins with contractility.
The sliding filament theory proposes that cross bridges between myosin and actin filaments cause muscle contraction.
Release of Ca++ from sarcoplasmic reticulum and binding of actin to myosin head causes cross bridges and muscle contraction.

concepts.concept_title: Red and White Muscle Fibre Classification
concepts.description: Muscle fibres are classified as red and white based on the amount of myoglobin pigment.
The skeletal system consists of bones and cartilages, divisible into axial and appendicular skeleton.
Three types of joints are formed between bones or between bone and cartilage – fibrous, cartilaginous, and synovial.
Synovial joints allow considerable movements and therefore, play a significant role in locomotion.
A diagram of a sarcomere of skeletal muscle showing different regions.

The coordination of organ systems is the process by which two or more organs interact and complement each other's functions. This process helps maintain homeostasis and ensure the efficient functioning of the body.

The human neural system is divided into two parts: the central neural system (CNS) and the peripheral neural system (PNS). The CNS includes the brain and spinal cord, while the PNS comprises nerves associated with the CNS.

The peripheral neural system has two types of nerve fibres: afferent and efferent. Afferent fibres transmit impulses from tissues to the CNS, while efferent fibres transmit regulatory impulses from the CNS to peripheral tissues.

The peripheral neural system is further divided into somatic and autonomic neural systems. The somatic neural system transmits impulses to skeletal muscles, while the autonomic neural system transmits impulses to involuntary organs and smooth muscles.
A neuron is composed of a cell body, dendrites, and an axon. The cell body contains cytoplasm and Nissl's granules, while dendrites and axons transmit and receive impulses.
The neural system enables quick coordination through point-to-point connections, allowing impulses to be transmitted rapidly between neurons.
Synapses are junctions between neurons where impulse conduction takes place, enabling chemical integration through neurotransmitters.
The visceral nervous system is a complex of nerves, fibres, ganglia, and plexuses that transmit impulses to and from the viscera.
A neuron consists of a dendrite, cell body, and axon. The axon has synaptic knob containing synaptic vesicles.
Neurons are divided into multipolar, bipolar, and unipolar based on the number and types of axons and dendrites.
Myelinated and non-myelinated axons have distinct structures with myelinated having Schwann cells and nodes of Ranvier.
The resting membrane is polarized due to different ion channels and ion concentrations. Application of a stimulus causes depolarization.
An action potential is generated at a site when the membrane becomes freely permeable to Na+, leading to a rapid influx of Na+.
The axoplasm has high K+ and low Na+ concentration, while the external fluid has low K+ and high Na+ concentration.
The sodium-potassium pump maintains ionic gradients across the resting membrane by transporting 3 Na+ outwards for 2 K+.
The impulse is conducted through the axon due to the rise in permeability to Na+ and subsequent decrease in permeability to K+. This leads to a rapid change in the membrane potential.
After the impulse has passed, the excess K+ flows out of the membrane, restoring the resting potential and making the axon responsive to further stimulation.

concepts.concept_title: Synaptic Transmission: Electrical vs. Chemical Synapses
concepts.description: Impulse transmission between neurons can occur through direct electrical synapses or chemical synapses involving neurotransmitters.
Chemical synapses involve the release of neurotransmitters from pre-synaptic neuron vesicles, which bind to specific receptors on the post-synaptic membrane, generating a new potential.
Neurotransmitters can be excitatory or inhibitory, influencing the development of the new potential in the post-synaptic neuron.
The brain controls voluntary movements, involuntary organ function, thermoregulation, circadian rhythms, and human behavior.
The human brain can be divided into three major parts: forebrain, midbrain, and hindbrain.
The forebrain consists of cerebrum, thalamus, and hypothalamus, with the cerebrum being divided into two halves by a deep cleft.

The resting potential is the electrical potential difference across the resting neural membrane, while a nerve impulse is conducted along the axon membrane in the form of a wave of depolarisation and repolarisation.
A synapse is formed by the membranes of a pre-synaptic neuron and a post-synaptic neuron, which may or may not be separated by a gap called the synaptic cleft.
Neurotransmitters are chemicals involved in the transmission of impulses at chemical synapses.

concepts.concept_title: Components of the Human Neural System
concepts.description: The human neural system consists of the central neural system (CNS) and the peripheral neural system, with the CNS comprising the brain and spinal cord.
The brain can be divided into three major parts: forebrain, midbrain, and hindbrain, with the forebrain consisting of the cerebrum, thalamus, and hypothalamus.
The limbic system is involved in the regulation of sexual behaviour, expression of emotional reactions, and motivation.

concepts.concept_title: Structure and Function of the Midbrain
concepts.description: The midbrain receives and integrates visual, tactile, and auditory inputs, and is located between the thalamus/hypothalamus of the forebrain and the pons of the hindbrain.

concepts.concept_title: Structure and Function of the Hindbrain
concepts.description: The hindbrain comprises the pons, cerebellum, and medulla, with the cerebellum integrating information received from the brain and the medulla controlling respiration, cardiovascular reflexes, and gastric secretions.
The brain stem forms the connections between the brain and spinal cord and comprises the midbrain, pons, and medulla oblongata.
The brain is divided into three main parts: forebrain, midbrain, and hindbrain.

concepts.concept_title: Central Neural System (CNS) and Peripheral Neural System (PNS)
concepts.description: CNS consists of the brain and spinal cord, while PNS consists of nerves that branch out from the CNS.
Resting potential is the stable voltage difference across a neuron's membrane, while action potential is a sudden change in voltage.
The membrane of a nerve fibre becomes polarised due to the difference in concentration of ions.
Depolarisation occurs when the membrane becomes permeable to certain ions, leading to a change in voltage.

concepts.concept_title: Transmission of Nerve Impulse across Chemical Synapse
concepts.description: A neurotransmitter is released from the presynaptic neuron and binds to receptors on the postsynaptic neuron.
A neuron consists of a cell body, dendrites, and an axon.
Myelinated axons have a fatty insulating layer, while non-myelinated axons do not.
Dendrites receive signals, while axons transmit signals.
Thalamus acts as a relay station, while hypothalamus regulates body temperature and hunger.
Cerebrum is the seat of higher brain functions, while cerebellum coordinates muscle movements.
A neurotransmitter is released from the presynaptic neuron and binds to receptors on the postsynaptic neuron.

concepts.concept_title: Role of Na+ in Action Potential Generation
concepts.description: Sodium ions play a crucial role in generating action potential by increasing the permeability of the membrane.
Afferent neurons carry signals to the brain, while efferent neurons carry signals away from the brain.
Myelinated nerve fibres conduct signals faster than unmyelinated fibre.
Cranial nerves arise from the brain, while spinal nerves arise from the spinal cord.
The endocrine system is a unique system that uses hormones as messengers to coordinate and regulate physiological functions in the body.
Endocrine glands are ductless glands that produce hormones in trace amounts, which are then transported to target organs.
The hypothalamus regulates a wide spectrum of body functions and stimulates or inhibits the synthesis and secretion of pituitary hormones.

concepts.concept_title: Releasing and Inhibiting Hormones of Hypothalamus
concepts.description: The hypothalamus produces releasing hormones that stimulate pituitary hormone secretion and inhibiting hormones that suppress pituitary hormone release.
The pituitary gland is divided into an adenohypophysis and a neurohypophysis, with the adenohypophysis consisting of pars distalis and pars intermedia.
Hypothyroidism in pregnant women leads to cretinism in babies, stunted growth, mental retardation, and abnormal skin.

concepts.concept_title: Hormonal Regulation of Basal Metabolic Rate
concepts.description: Thyroid hormones regulate the basal metabolic rate and support red blood cell formation.

concepts.concept_title: Control of Carbohydrate, Protein, and Fat Metabolism
concepts.description: Thyroid hormones control the metabolism of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats.

concepts.concept_title: Maintenance of Water and Electrolyte Balance
concepts.description: Thyroid hormones influence the maintenance of water and electrolyte balance in the body.
Thyrocalcitonin regulates the blood calcium levels in the body.
Parathyroid hormone secretion is regulated by the circulating levels of calcium ions.
Parathyroid hormone increases the levels of calcium ions in the blood by stimulating bone resorption and increasing calcium absorption from food.

concepts.concept_title: Role of Parathyroid Hormone in Calcium Balance
concepts.description: Parathyroid hormone plays a significant role in maintaining calcium balance in the body.
The thymus gland plays a major role in the development of the immune system by secreting thymosins.
Adrenal medulla secretes adrenaline and noradrenaline, which are emergency hormones that increase alertness and stimulate the breakdown of glycogen.
Underproduction of hormones by the adrenal cortex can lead to Addison’s disease, causing weakness, fatigue, and altered carbohydrate metabolism.

The adrenal cortex secretes many hormones called corticoids, which are divided into glucocorticoids and mineralocorticoids. Glucocorticoids are involved in carbohydrate metabolism, while mineralocorticoids regulate water and electrolyte balance.

Glucocorticoids stimulate gluconeogenesis, lipolysis, and proteolysis, while inhibiting cellular uptake and utilization of amino acids. They also maintain cardiovascular function and regulate immune response.
Mineralocorticoids, such as aldosterone, regulate electrolyte balance and maintain blood pressure by stimulating reabsorption of Na+ and water, and excretion of K+ and phosphate ions.
The adrenal cortex secretes small amounts of androgenic steroids, which regulate growth of male secondary sex characteristics.

Islets of Langerhans are composed of α-cells and β-cells, which secrete glucagon and insulin, respectively. Glucagon maintains normal blood glucose levels, while insulin regulates glucose homeostasis.
Glucagon acts on liver cells, stimulating glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis, and reducing cellular glucose uptake and utilization.
Insulin acts on liver and adipose tissue cells, enhancing glucose uptake and utilization, and stimulating glycogenesis.
Prolonged hyperglycemia leads to diabetes mellitus, a disorder characterized by glucose loss through urine and formation of ketone bodies.

concepts.concept_title: Role of Androgens in Male Sexual Development
concepts.description: Androgens stimulate the formation of spermatozoa, influence male sexual behavior, and produce anabolic effects on protein and carbohydrate metabolism.

concepts.concept_title: Function of Estrogen in Female Organisms
concepts.description: Estrogen stimulates the growth and activity of female secondary sex organs, development of ovarian follicles, and regulation of female sexual behavior.

concepts.concept_title: Role of Progesterone in Pregnancy and Mammary Gland Development
concepts.description: Progesterone supports pregnancy, stimulates the formation of alveoli in mammary glands, and milk secretion.

concepts.concept_title: Secretion and Function of Atrial Natriuretic Factor (ANF)
concepts.description: ANF is secreted by the atrial wall of the heart, causes dilation of blood vessels, and decreases blood pressure.
Erythropoietin is produced by juxtaglomerular cells of the kidney, stimulates erythropoiesis, and formation of RBC.

concepts.concept_title: Secretion of Gastric and Pancreatic Hormones
concepts.description: Gastrin, secretin, cholecystokinin (CCK), and gastric inhibitory peptide (GIP) are secreted by endocrine cells in the gastrointestinal tract, stimulating secretion of digestive enzymes and hormones.
Hormones bind to specific receptors in target tissues, leading to the formation of a hormone-receptor complex, which causes biochemical changes and regulates target tissue metabolism.

concepts.concept_title: Classification of Hormones based on Chemical Nature
concepts.description: Hormones can be classified into four groups: peptide, polypeptide, protein hormones; steroids; iodothyronines (thyroid hormones); and other types.
Hormones that interact with membrane-bound receptors generate second messengers that regulate cellular metabolism. Examples include cyclic AMP and IP.
Hormones that interact with intracellular receptors regulate gene expression or chromosome function.
Hormones can be classified based on their chemical structure and mechanism of action.
The pineal gland secretes melatonin, which regulates 24-hour rhythms of the body, including sleep and wakefulness.
Thyroid gland hormones regulate basal metabolic rate, development and maturation of the central nervous system, and erythropoiesis.
The parathyroid glands secrete parathyroid hormone (PTH), which regulates calcium levels in the blood.
The adrenal gland secretes epinephrine and norepinephrine, which increase alertness and stimulate various physiological processes.
The endocrine pancreas secretes glucagon and insulin, which regulate glucose metabolism and blood sugar levels.
The testis secretes androgens, which stimulate male reproductive functions and development.
The ovary secretes estrogen and progesterone, which regulate female reproductive functions and development.
Exocrine glands secrete their products onto the surface of the organ, whereas endocrine glands secrete hormones into the bloodstream.

concepts.concept_title: Endocrine system and its location in human body
concepts.description: Endocrine glands are located at various sites in the body including the hypothalamus, pituitary, thyroid, parathyroid, adrenal glands, pancreas, testes, ovaries, thymus, and atrium.

concepts.concept_title: Hormones Secreted by Different Endocrine Glands
concepts.description: Various endocrine glands secrete specific hormones, such as: hypophysis, thyroid, parathyroid, adrenal, pancreas, testis, and ovary.
Hormones are secreted by various glands and target specific glands, such as hypothalamic hormones (adenohypophysis), TSH (thyroid gland), ACTH (adrenal cortex), and LH and FSH (gonads).

Different hormones have various functions, such as parathyroid hormone regulating calcium metabolism, thyroid hormones regulating metabolism, thymosin regulating immune development, androgens and estrogens regulating reproductive functions, and insulin and glucagon regulating blood glucose levels.

concepts.concept_title: Examples of Hormones with Specific Functions
concepts.description: Examples of hormones include: hyperglycemic hormone (glucagon) and hypoglycemic hormone (insulin), hypercalcemic hormone (PTH), gonadotrophic hormones (LH and FSH), progestational hormone (progesterone), and blood pressure lowering hormone (atrial natriuretic peptide).
Hormonal deficiencies can lead to various disorders, such as diabetes mellitus (insulin deficiency), goitre (thyroid hormone deficiency), and cretinism (thyroid hormone deficiency).
FSH stimulates follicular growth in the ovaries, leading to ovulation and follicular maturation.

concepts.concept_title: Matching the Hormones with Their Functions
concepts.description: Hormones such as T, PTH, GnRH, and LH can be matched with their functions in the endocrine system.
The male reproductive system is located in the pelvis region and includes a pair of testes, accessory ducts, glands, and external genitalia.
The scrotum helps in maintaining the low temperature of the testes necessary for spermatogenesis.
Each testis has about 250 compartments called testicular lobules, each containing one to three highly coiled seminiferous tubules.

concepts.concept_title: Function of Spermatogonia and Sertoli Cells
concepts.description: Male germ cells (spermatogonia) undergo meiotic divisions, while Sertoli cells provide nutrition to the germ cells.
Leydig cells synthesise and secrete testicular hormones called androgens.
The male sex accessory ducts include rete testis, vasa efferentia, epididymis, and vas deferens.
The testes are located in the scrotum and contain seminiferous tubules where sperms are produced. Each testis is connected to an epididymis which stores and transports sperms.

concepts.concept_title: Path of Sperms in Male Reproductive System
concepts.description: Sperms are transported from the testes to the urethra through the epididymis, vas deferens, and seminal vesicles.
The penis is the male external genitalia made up of special tissue that helps in erection. It consists of the glans penis, foreskin, corpora cavernosa, and spongy tissue.
The male accessory glands include paired seminal vesicles, a prostate, and paired bulbourethral glands which secrete seminal plasma.
Ovaries are the primary female sex organs that produce ova and several steroid hormones.
A sperm is a microscopic structure composed of a head, neck, a middle piece, and a tail. It contains an elongated haploid nucleus and enzymes in the acrosome.
For normal fertility, at least 60 per cent sperms must have normal shape and size and at least 40 per cent of them must show vigorous motility.

concepts.concept_title: Role of Male Sex Accessory Ducts and Glands
concepts.description: Male sex accessory ducts and glands produce secretions essential for sperm maturation and motility. These secretions constitute the semen.
Oogenesis is the process of formation of a mature female gamete, which is markedly different from spermatogenesis.
Primary oocytes are formed during embryonic development when a couple of million gamete mother cells (oogonia) are formed within each fetal ovary.
Primary follicles get surrounded by more layers of granulosa cells and a new theca, forming secondary follicles.
A tertiary follicle is characterized by a fluid-filled cavity called an antrum, and the theca layer is organised into an inner theca interna and an outer theca externa.

concepts.concept_title: Unequal Meiotic Division and Polar Body Formation
concepts.description: Primary oocyte completes its first meiotic division, resulting in the formation of a large haploid secondary oocyte and a tiny first polar body.
Gonadotropins LH and FSH stimulate follicular development and secretion of estrogens by the growing follicles.
Rapid secretion of LH leading to its maximum level during the mid-cycle, known as LH surge, induces ovulation.
The remaining parts of the Graafian follicle transform into the corpus luteum, which secretes large amounts of progesterone.

concepts.concept_title: Implantation of Fertilized Ovum and Pregnancy
concepts.description: An endometrium is necessary for implantation of the fertilized ovum and other events of pregnancy.
Corpus luteum degenerates after ovulation, leading to the disintegration of endometrium and menstruation.
Cyclic menstruation is an indicator of normal reproductive phase and extends between menarche and menopause.
Semen is released by the penis into the vagina during copulation and motile sperms pass through the cervix and enter into the uterus.
Ovum is transported to the ampullary region of the fallopian tube where fertilisation takes place.
The fusion of a sperm with an ovum is called fertilisation, which occurs when a sperm comes in contact with the zona pellucida layer of the ovum.
The zona pellucida layer of the ovum blocks the entry of additional sperms after contact with a sperm, ensuring only one sperm fertilises an ovum.
Fertilisation can only occur if the ovum and sperms are transported simultaneously to the ampullary region of the fallopian tube.
Not all copulations lead to fertilisation and pregnancy due to ovum and sperms not being transported simultaneously.

concepts.concept_title: Fertilisation Process and Sperm Entry into Ovum
concepts.description: The secretions of the acrosome help the sperm enter into the cytoplasm of the ovum through the zona pellucida and plasma membrane.
The division of blastomeres is called a morula, which continues to divide and transforms into a blastocyst.
The trophoblast layer differentiates into an outer layer, and the inner cell mass differentiates as the embryo.
After implantation, the uterine cells divide rapidly and cover the blastocyst, leading to pregnancy.

The chorionic villi and uterine tissue become interdigitated with each other to form a placenta, facilitating the supply of oxygen and nutrients to the embryo and removal of excretory/waste materials.
The umbilical cord helps in the transport of substances to and from the embryo.
The placenta produces several hormones like hCG, hPL, relaxin, estrogens, and progestogens.
Hormones support fetal growth, metabolic changes in the mother, and maintenance of pregnancy.

concepts.concept_title: Inner Cell Mass and Ectoderm, Endoderm, and Mesoderm Formation
concepts.description: The inner cell mass differentiates into ectoderm, endoderm, and mesoderm, which form all tissues in adults.
Stem cells in the inner cell mass have the potency to give rise to all tissues and organs.
Major features of embryonic development occur at various months of pregnancy, including heart formation, limb and digit development, and organ system formation.
The average duration of human pregnancy is about 9 months, and parturition is induced by a complex neuroendocrine mechanism.
Oxytocin triggers release of further oxytocin and induces stronger uterine contractions, leading to foetal ejection reflex.
The process in which a baby is expelled out of the uterus through the birth canal.
The mammary glands of the female undergo differentiation during pregnancy.
The process of milk production by the mammary glands towards the end of pregnancy.
The milk produced during the initial few days of lactation, containing several antibodies essential for new-born babies.
Humans are sexually reproducing and viviparous.
The male reproductive system consists of testes, male sex accessory ducts, accessory glands, and external genitalia.
The seminiferous tubules are coiled structures within the testes, lined by spermatogonia and Sertoli cells.
The process of sperm formation through meiotic divisions of spermatogonia.
Leydig cells outside the seminiferous tubules synthesize and secrete testicular hormones called androgens.
The female reproductive system consists of ovaries, oviducts, uterus, vagina, external genitalia, and mammary glands.
Ovarian follicles in different stages of development are embedded in the stroma.
Spermatogenesis results in the formation of sperms, whereas oogenesis leads to the formation of mature female gametes.
The cyclical changes in the ovary and the uterus during the menstrual cycle are induced by changes in the levels of pituitary and ovarian hormones.
The presence of X or Y chromosome in the sperm determines the sex of the offspring.
The human ovary releases a relatively small number of eggs in a month. This number remains the same whether identical or fraternal twins are born.

concepts.concept_title: Number of Eggs Released in Twin Pregnancy
concepts.description: The number of eggs released from the ovary remains the same regardless of whether the twins are identical or fraternal, except in the case of identical twins being from a single fertilized egg.
In a polycygous pregnancy of 6 puppies, the number of eggs released by the ovary of a female dog is the same as in a normal pregnancy, which is typically 1 egg per month.
According to WHO, reproductive health refers to a total well-being in all aspects of reproduction, including physical, emotional, behavioural, and social.
Maintaining reproductive health is essential for society, and methods are taken up to achieve it, involving programmes and awareness creation.
Reproductive health includes physical, emotional, behavioural, and social aspects of reproduction.
India initiated family planning programmes in 1951 and later introduced RCH programmes for total reproductive health.
Sex education in schools helps to provide right information to the young, leading to a healthy lifestyle and discouraging myths and misconceptions.
RCH programmes involve awareness creation about reproductive health-related aspects through media and dissemination by various groups.
Awareness and implementation of measures to promote reproductive health, prevent population growth problems, and maintain a healthy society.
Importance of strong infrastructural facilities, professional expertise, and material support in providing medical assistance and care for reproductive health-related problems.
Strategies for implementing action plans to attain reproductive health, including better techniques and new strategies for providing care and assistance.
The procedure of amniocentesis, its use for sex determination, and related issues like female foeticides.
Importance of research in various reproduction-related areas to find new methods and improve existing ones.
Factors contributing to population growth, including improved health facilities and living conditions, and their impact on population size.
The population growth rate is a measure of the rate at which a population increases over time, expressed as a percentage of the total population per year.
Contraceptive methods are used to prevent unwanted pregnancies and are typically categorized into Natural/Traditional, Barrier, IUDs, Oral contraceptives, Injectables, Implants, and Surgical methods.
Natural/Traditional contraceptive methods, such as Periodic abstinence, Withdrawal, and Lactational amenorrhea, work on the principle of avoiding chances of ovum and sperms meeting.
Barrier contraceptive methods, such as condoms, prevent sperm from reaching the egg by creating a physical barrier between the two.
An ideal contraceptive should be user-friendly, effective, reversible, and have minimal side effects, without interfering with the user's sexual drive or act.
Methods that prevent physical meeting of gametes, such as condoms, diaphragms, and IUDs.
Male and female condoms, disposable and reusable, that can be self-inserted for privacy.
Spermicidal creams, jellies, and foams used with diaphragms and cervical caps to increase contraceptive efficiency.
Non-medicated and medicated IUDs that increase phagocytosis of sperms and suppress sperm motility and fertilizing capacity.
Vasectomy and tubectomy procedures that block gamete transport and thereby prevent conception.
Pregnancy termination by qualified medical professionals on certain grounds, such as within 12 weeks of conception.
MTP is the intentional or voluntary termination of pregnancy before full term, also known as induced abortion. It is performed to prevent risk to the life of the pregnant woman or the child.

MTP can be performed if the continuation of pregnancy would involve risk to the life of the pregnant woman or the child, or if there is a substantial risk that the child would be born with significant physical or mental abnormalities.
MTP is relatively safe during the first trimester, but second trimester abortions are riskier. Illegal and unqualified abortions are unsafe and can be fatal.
MTP should not be performed solely to determine the sex of the unborn child. Misuse of MTP and amniocentesis should be avoided as these practices are dangerous for the mother and the foetus.
STI is an infection or disease that is transmitted through sexual contact. Effective prevention and treatment are essential to avoid the transmission and complications of STI.

STIs are infections transmitted through sexual intercourse or other means, including sharing needles and blood transfusion, and can cause symptoms such as pain, discharge, and swelling in the genital region.
STIs can be transmitted through sexual intercourse, sharing needles, blood transfusion, and from an infected mother to the foetus.
Untreated STIs can lead to complications such as pelvic inflammatory diseases, abortions, stillbirths, ectopic pregnancies, infertility, and cancer of the reproductive tract.
STIs can be prevented by avoiding sex with unknown partners, using condoms, and getting timely detection and treatment from a qualified doctor.
Infertility refers to a couple's inability to produce children despite unprotected sexual co-habitation, with reasons including physical, congenital, and psychological factors.
IVF is a process where ova from a woman or donor and sperms from a man or donor fuse to form zygote outside the body, and then transferred into the fallopian tube or uterus for further development.
ZIFT is a technique where zygote or early embryos are transferred into the fallopian tube for further development.
IUT is a technique where embryos with more than 8 blastomeres are transferred into the uterus for further development.
GIFT is a technique where an ovum collected from a donor is transferred into the fallopian tube of another female who cannot produce one.
ICSI is a technique where a sperm is directly injected into the ovum to form an embryo in the laboratory.
AI is a technique where semen collected from the husband or a healthy donor is artificially introduced into the vagina or uterus of the female.
IUI is a technique where semen is artificially introduced into the uterus of the female.
Reproductive health refers to a total well-being in all aspects of reproduction, physical, emotional, behavioural and social.
STIs, including AIDS, are a primary concern for reproductive health. They can lead to complications like Pelvic Inflammatory Diseases (PIDs), stillbirth, and infertility.

MTP is legalized in India for cases where the pregnancy is unwanted due to rape, casual relationships, or if the continuation of pregnancy could be harmful or fatal to either the mother or the foetus.
PIDs are a complication of sexually transmitted diseases. Early detection facilitates better cure of these diseases.

concepts.concept_title: Infections or Diseases Transmitted through Sexual Intercourse
concepts.description: Sexually transmitted diseases (STIs) are infections or diseases transmitted through sexual intercourse.
Inability to conceive or produce children after 2 years of unprotected sexual cohabitation is called infertility. Various methods, such as In Vitro Fertilization, are available to help such couples.

concepts.concept_title: Contraindications for Removal of Gonads as a Contraceptive Option
concepts.description: Removal of gonads cannot be considered as a contraceptive option because of its harmful effects.
Precautions such as avoiding sexual intercourse with unknown/multiple partners, use of condoms, can help prevent contracting STDs.
Occurrences of abortions even without external intervention are known as spontaneous abortions.
Infertility is the inability to produce a viable offspring, and it is not always due to female partner abnormalities.
Complete lactation may not be entirely effective as a method of contraception, as the frequency of sexual intercourse can be controlled.
Creating awareness about sex-related aspects can improve reproductive health.
Surgical methods of contraception do not prevent gamete formation, but they may prevent ovulation or fertilization.
Not all sexually transmitted diseases are completely curable.
Oral contraceptive pills are not equally popular among rural women due to various socio-economic factors.
In Embryo Transfer (ET) techniques, embryos are not always transferred into the uterus, as some may be discarded.
Understanding of the genetic basis of inheritance and the conversion of genotype to phenotype.
The double-helical structure of DNA and its components.

concepts.concept_title: Watson and Crick's Work on DNA
concepts.description: The discovery of the double-helical structure of DNA by Watson and Crick.
The idea of inheritance patterns and the factors that determine the phenotype.
Inheritance is the process by which characters are passed on from parent to progeny; it is the basis of heredity.
Variation is the degree by which progeny differ from their parents.
Genes are the units of inheritance, containing information required to express a particular trait in an organism.
Alleles are slightly different forms of the same gene, used to express contrasting traits.
Capital and lower-case letters are used to represent dominant and recessive alleles, respectively.
Genotype refers to the genetic makeup of an organism, while phenotype refers to its physical appearance.
One allele can dominate over the other, resulting in the dominant allele expressing the trait, while the recessive allele does not.
In genetics, alleles can be either identical (homozygote) or different (heterozygote).
Punnett Square is a tool used to predict the probability of different genotypes and phenotypes in a monohybrid cross.
In genetics, dominant alleles will be expressed in a given phenotype even when an individual is heterozygous.

concepts.concept_title: Phenotypic and Genotypic Ratios in Monohybrid Cross
concepts.description: In a monohybrid cross, the phenotypic ratio can be 3:1, while the genotypic ratio can be 1:2:1.
Binomial expression (ax + by) can be used to calculate the probability of different genotypes and phenotypes in a monohybrid cross.
In genetics, a test cross is used to determine the genotype of an individual with a dominant phenotype.
The law states that in a pair of alleles, one member dominates the other, resulting in the expression of only one of the parental characters in the F1 generation.
The law explains that alleles segregate from each other during gamete formation, resulting in gametes receiving only one of the two factors.
A dominant allele produces a functional enzyme, while a recessive allele may produce a non-functional enzyme or no enzyme, resulting in the loss of the original phenotype.
In co-dominance, the F1 generation resembles both parents, resulting from the expression of more than one allele in the phenotype.

Codominance occurs when two different alleles of a gene result in both alleles being expressed. In the case of ABO blood types, this means that the 'A' and 'B' alleles are both expressed when present together, resulting in type AB blood.

When a single gene has more than two alleles, different genotypes can result from the various combinations of these alleles. In the case of ABO blood types, there are three alleles that can produce six different genotypes.
The possible phenotypes that can result from multiple alleles are determined by how the alleles interact with each other and how they are expressed in the phenotype.
Incomplete dominance occurs when a heterozygous individual exhibits a phenotype that is neither the dominant nor the recessive trait, but rather an intermediate or blended form of the two.
Dominance is not an autonomous feature of a gene or its product, but rather depends on how the gene product and the production of a particular phenotype interact.
In monohybrid crosses, the dominant allele can be identified by its ability to be expressed over the recessive allele, and this dominance can be observed in the phenotype of the offspring.
In Mendel's dihybrid cross experiment, segregation of one pair of genes is independent of the other pair, resulting in a 9:3:3:1 ratio of phenotypes.

A square used to predict the probability of genotypes and phenotypes formed by the combination of two pairs of alleles, with each allele having two alleles and a 25% frequency of four types of gametes.

concepts.concept_title: Genotypic and Phenotypic Ratios in Dihybrid Cross
concepts.description: The genotypic and phenotypic ratios in a dihybrid cross can be derived using Punnett squares, with a 9:3:3:1 ratio observed for several pairs of characters.
A generalisation that states that segregation of one pair of characters is independent of the other pair of characters when two pairs of traits are combined in a hybrid.
Mendel's work on inheritance was initially unrecognized due to communication difficulties and the unacceptance of his concept of genes as stable units.
During gamete formation, chromosomes segregate, ensuring one of each pair is transmitted, and independent pairs segregate independently of each other.
Chromosomes, as the physical manifestation of genes, double and divide during cell division, demonstrating their role in inheritance.
Two alleles of a gene are located on homologous chromosomes at homologous sites, illustrating the genetic basis of inheritance.
During meiosis, chromosomes segregate, leading to the formation of gametes, showcasing the process of reduction division.

concepts.concept_title: Chromosome Alignment and Anaphase I Segregation
concepts.description: During Anaphase of meiosis I, chromosomes align and segregate, illustrating the mechanism behind the production of genetically unique gametes.
Chromosomes segregate independently of each other during meiosis.
Genes that are close together on a chromosome tend to be inherited together.
Traits like flower color are controlled by single genes with distinct alternate forms.
Traits like height and skin color are controlled by multiple genes and are influenced by environment.
In polygenic traits, the effect of each allele is additive, resulting in a range of phenotypes.
A single gene can exhibit multiple phenotypic expressions.
Inheritance of a trait or disease due to a single mutated gene, such as phenylketonuria caused by mutation in the phenylalanine hydroxylase gene.

concepts.concept_title: Chromosomal Basis of Sex Determination in Insects
concepts.description: The discovery of a specific nuclear structure, X body, in insects and its role in sex determination.
The presence of two different sex chromosomes, Z and W, in bird species, resulting in female heterogamety.
The genetic makeup of the sperm determines the sex of the child, resulting in a 50% probability of either a male or female offspring.
In honey bees, females are diploid (32 chromosomes), males are haploid (16 chromosomes), and males produce sperms by mitosis.
A point mutation is a change in a single base pair of DNA, such as in sickle cell anemia.
A frameshift mutation occurs due to deletions or insertions of base pairs of DNA.
Mutagens are chemical or physical factors that induce mutations in organisms.
Pedigree analysis is a method of studying the inheritance pattern of traits in human beings by analyzing family history.
Chromosomal aberrations are abnormalities or aberrations that result from alterations in chromosomes.
A graphical representation of a family tree to study the inheritance of a particular trait over generations.
An alteration or change in the genetic material that occurs occasionally.
Genetic disorders determined by alteration or mutation in a single gene.
Characteristics that can be inherited in a dominant (expressed) or recessive (hidden) manner.
Disorders caused by mutation in genes on the X chromosome, often affecting males more frequently than females.
Traits that can be inherited in a dominant or recessive manner, not linked to the sex chromosomes.
A sex-linked recessive disorder caused by a defect in the X chromosome that affects colour vision.
Recessive traits are expressed only when an individual has two recessive alleles, one from each parent. The affected individual does not express the trait if they have one dominant allele.
X-linked recessive diseases are caused by recessive genes located on the X chromosome. Females are often carriers and can transmit the disease to their sons, but it's rare for females to be affected.
An individual's genotype is the genetic makeup, while their phenotype is the physical expression of their genotype. Heterozygous individuals can be carriers of a disease or exhibit a trait.
Haemophilia can be transmitted from carrier females to their sons, but it's extremely rare for females to be affected, requiring both parents to be carrier and affected
Sickle-cell anaemia is an autosomal recessive trait caused by a point mutation that leads to polymerization under low oxygen tension, causing elongated RBCs and sickle-like structure.
Autosomal recessive traits are inherited when individuals have two recessive alleles, one from each parent. Parents can be carriers or affected, and offspring may be carriers or affected.
Phenylketonuria and Thalassemia are autosomal recessive diseases, inborn errors of metabolism, and blood disorders that are transmitted similarly to other autosomal recessive traits.

Thalassemia can be classified into Alpha and Beta types due to the affected globin chain. It is caused by mutation or deletion of the HBA1, HBA2, and HBB genes leading to reduced or incorrect synthesis of haemoglobin.

Chromosomal disorders occur due to abnormal number or arrangement of chromosomes. They can result from failure of segregation or cytokinesis during cell division, leading to aneuploidy, polyploidy, trisomy, or monosomy.
Down syndrome is caused by the presence of an additional copy of chromosome number 21, resulting in trisomy 21.
Human cells typically have 46 chromosomes (23 pairs), with 22 pairs being autosomes and one pair being sex chromosomes.
Klinefelter’s Syndrome causes short stature due to underdeveloped physical and mental growth.
A characteristic palm crease is observed in individuals with Klinefelter’s Syndrome.

concepts.concept_title: Genetic Disorders Due to Sex Chromosome Composition
concepts.description: Disorders such as Klinefelter’s and Turner’s Syndrome are caused by abnormalities in sex chromosome composition.
Individuals with Klinefelter’s Syndrome exhibit feminized characteristics, such as gynaecomastia.
Klinefelter’s Syndrome is characterized by a karyotype of 47, XXY.

concepts.concept_title: Turner’s Syndrome Caused by Absence of X Chromosome
concepts.description: Turner’s Syndrome is caused by the absence of one X chromosome, resulting in a karyotype of 45, X0.

concepts.concept_title: Genetics as a Branch of Biology
concepts.description: Genetics is a branch of biology that deals with principles of inheritance and variation.
In the Law of Dominance, dominant characters are expressed when factors are in heterozygous condition.
In the Law of Segregation, characters segregate during the formation of gametes.
Some characters show incomplete dominance, resulting in a blending of traits in heterozygous condition.
Some characters show co-dominance, resulting in the equal expression of traits in heterozygous condition.
In the Law of Independent Assortment, factors independently assort and combine in all permutations and combinations.
Punnett Square is a square tabular form used to represent the different combinations of gametes.
The genotype refers to the factors (genes) regulating a character, while the phenotype refers to the physical expression of the character.
The theory extends Mendel’s laws by relating them to the segregation and assortment of chromosomes during meiosis.
Linked genes are those that are located on the same chromosome and do not follow Mendel’s law of independent assortment.
Recombination occurs when genes that are linked but located far apart on a chromosome cross over during meiosis.
Sex-linked genes are those that are linked to the sex chromosomes and can be inherited in a sex-specific manner.
Autosomes are chromosomes that are not sex chromosomes and are inherited in a sex-unchanged manner.
Point mutation is a change of a single base pair in DNA and can result in a change in the genetic code.
Sickle-cell anemia is a genetic disorder caused by a point mutation in the gene coding for beta-chain of hemoglobin.
Polyploidy is a mutation involving a change in the total number of chromosomes in an organism.
Aneuploidy is a mutation involving a change in the number of copies of a specific chromosome in an organism.
Karyotypes are diagrams that show the chromosomes and their arrangement in an organism’s cells.
T.H. Morgan's contribution to genetics includes the discovery of sex-linked inheritance and the idea of the gene as a physical entity.
Pedigree analysis is a technique used to study the inheritance of traits by examining the ancestry of individuals.
Sex determination in humans is based on the presence of the X and Y chromosomes, with females having XX and males having XY.

concepts.concept_title: Genotype of Parents and Offspring (Blood Group O)
concepts.description: The parents' genotypes and the possible genotypes of offspring can be worked out using the Punnett square, given the blood groups of the parents.
Co-dominance is the phenomenon where two alleles of a gene have equal effect on the phenotype, resulting in a combination of the two parental traits.
Incomplete dominance is the phenomenon where one allele does not completely mask the effect of the other allele, resulting in a intermediate phenotype.
Point mutation is a type of mutation where a single nucleotide base is replaced or deleted in a DNA sequence.
The chromosomal theory of inheritance proposes that genes are located on chromosomes and are responsible for inheritance.
Autosomal genetic disorders are conditions that occur due to mutations in genes located on autosomes, causing various symptoms.
DNA is a long polymer of deoxyribonucleotides, which is usually defined by the number of nucleotides or base pairs.
Nitrogenous bases can be classified into two types: Purines (Adenine and Guanine) and Pyrimidines (Cytosine and Thymine)
A nucleotide has three components: a nitrogenous base, a pentose sugar, and a phosphate group.
The length of DNA is usually defined by the number of nucleotides or base pairs present in it.
DNA replication is the process by which DNA makes an exact copy of itself.
The genetic code is a set of rules that determines the sequence of amino acids in proteins.
Transcription is the process by which DNA makes an RNA molecule.
Translation is the process by which an RNA molecule makes a protein.
A nitrogenous base is linked to the OH of 1'C pentose sugar through a N-glycosidic linkage to form a nucleoside.

concepts.concept_title: Phosphate group linkage to form a nucleotide
concepts.description: A phosphate group is linked to OH of 5'C of a nucleoside through phosphoester linkage to form a corresponding nucleotide.
Two nucleotides are linked through 3'-5' phosphodiester linkage to form a dinucleotide, and more nucleotides can be joined in such a manner to form a polynucleotide chain.

A polynucleotide chain has a free phosphate moiety at 5'-end of sugar, which is referred to as 5’-end of polynucleotide chain, and a sugar has a free OH of 3'C group, which is referred to as 3' -end of the polynucleotide chain.
Adenine pairs with Thymine, and Guanine pairs with Cytosine in DNA, with base ratios of Adenine:Thymine and Guanine:Cytosine being constant and equal to one.
The base pairing in DNA makes the strands complementary to each other, allowing the sequence of bases in one strand to be predicted from the sequence of bases in the other strand.
The Double-helix model of DNA consists of two polynucleotide chains with base pairing and complementary nature.
The DNA double helix is composed of two complementary sugar-phosphate chains with anti-parallel polarity.
Adenine pairs with thymine through two hydrogen bonds, while guanine pairs with cytosine through three hydrogen bonds.
Purines always pair with pyrimidines, creating a consistent distance between DNA strands.
The DNA double helix has a right-handed spiral structure with 10 base pairs per turn.
The pitch of the DNA helix is approximately 3.4 nm, with a distance of 0.34 nm between consecutive base pairs.
In eukaryotic cells, DNA is wrapped around histone proteins to form nucleosomes, while in prokaryotes, DNA is organized into nucleoids.
A nucleosome is formed by 200 bp of DNA wrapped around a histone octamer, creating a 'beads-on-string' structure.
Chromatin is composed of repeating nucleosomes, forming a thread-like structure in the nucleus.
Chromatin is packaged into chromatin fibers, which are coiled and condensed into chromosomes during cell division.
Euchromatin is loosely packed and transcriptionally active chromatin, while heterochromatin is densely packed and transcriptionally inactive chromatin.
Non-histone chromosomal proteins (NHC) play a crucial role in packaging chromatin at higher levels.

concepts.concept_title: TSGM and the Hunt for Genetic Material
concepts.description: The search for genetic material narrowed down to chromosomes, but the actual molecule responsible for heredity was not identified until later.
The transforming principle refers to the unknown genetic material responsible for heredity, discovered by Frederick Griffith in his experiments with Streptococcus pneumoniae.
Avery, MacLeod, and McCarty determined the biochemical nature of the genetic material, previously thought to be a protein.
Griffith's experiment where DNA from Streptococcus pneumoniae converted heat-killed R. pneumoniae cells into S. pneumoniae cells.
DNA from Streptococcus pneumoniae causes transformation of R. pneumoniae cells into S. pneumoniae cells.
DNase digestion inhibits DNA transformation, indicating that DNA is the transforming substance.
The genetic material is the molecule responsible for transmission of genetic traits.
Alfred Hershey and Martha Chase used radioactive phosphorus and sulfur to prove that DNA is the genetic material.

concepts.concept_title: Properties of Genetic Material: DNA vs RNA
concepts.description: DNA acts as the genetic material in most organisms, but RNA can also act as genetic material in some viruses.
A molecule must be able to replicate, be stable chemically and structurally, allow for mutation, and express itself as 'Mendelian Characters'.

concepts.concept_title: Rule of base pairing and complementarity
concepts.description: Both DNA and RNA can direct their duplications due to the rule of base pairing and complementarity.
Genetic material should be stable enough not to change with different stages of life cycle, age, or with change in physiology of the organism.
DNA is less reactive and structurally more stable than RNA due to the absence of the 2'-OH group.

concepts.concept_title: Thymine and uracil in DNA and RNA
concepts.description: The presence of thymine in DNA confers additional stability compared to uracil in RNA.

concepts.concept_title: Mutation rate in DNA and RNA
concepts.description: RNA mutates at a faster rate than DNA due to its instability.

concepts.concept_title: Expression of genetic information in DNA and RNA
concepts.description: DNA expresses genetic information through RNA, which can directly code for protein synthesis.

concepts.concept_title: RNA and DNA as genetic material
concepts.description: Both RNA and DNA can function as genetic material, but DNA is preferred for storing genetic information, while RNA is better for transmission.
RNA was the first genetic material and was involved in essential life processes such as metabolism and translation.
RNA used to act as a catalyst in some biochemical reactions, but its reactivity made it unstable.
DNA evolved from RNA with chemical modifications that made it more stable and resistant to changes.
The double helical structure of DNA, proposed by Watson and Crick, is characterized by double-stranded and complementary strands.
The Watson-Crick model proposed that DNA replicates semiconservatively, with the two strands acting as templates for the synthesis of new strands.
The Meselson and Stahl experiment demonstrated that DNA replicates semiconservatively by separating DNA molecules based on their density.
Molecules with higher mass/density sediment faster under centrifugal force, allowing for separation based on density.
Semiconservative DNA replication involves the replication of DNA with one parental and one newly syntheisized strand.
Experiments on E. coli and Vicia faba showed that DNA replicates semiconservatively, with equal amounts of old and new DNA.
DNA-dependent DNA polymerase is a main enzyme required for DNA replication, using a DNA template to synthesize new DNA.
DNA replication requires highly efficient and accurate enzymes to synthesize a large number of nucleotides in a short time.
DNA replication is an energetically expensive process requiring high-energy phosphates and energy from substrates.
Replication occurs within a replication fork, involving enzymes like DNA-dependent DNA polymerase, DNA ligase, and requiring a replication origin.
Replication initiation requires a specific region called the replication origin, and the process cannot start randomly.

concepts.concept_title: DNA replication and cell division coordination
concepts.description: DNA replication and cell division are highly coordinated processes in eukaryotes, with failure resulting in polyploidy.
DNA transcription is the process of copying genetic information from one DNA strand into RNA, governed by complementarity.

concepts.concept_title: Principle of complementarity in DNA replication and transcription
concepts.description: The principle of complementarity dictates that in DNA replication, each strand acts as a template for the other strand, whereas in transcription, only one strand serves as a template.

concepts.concept_title: Single-stranded RNA as a result of transcription
concepts.description: During transcription, only one strand of DNA is copied into RNA, resulting in a single-stranded RNA molecule.

concepts.concept_title: Regions of a transcription unit in DNA
concepts.description: A transcription unit consists of three main regions: Promoter, Structural gene, and Terminator.

concepts.concept_title: Template and coding strand in DNA
concepts.description: The template strand has 3' to 5' polarity, while the coding strand has 5' to 3' polarity and the same sequence as RNA.
The coding strand is the strand of DNA that directly complementary to the mRNA produced.
The template strand is complementary to the coding strand and serves as a template for RNA synthesis.
The process by which DNA is transcribed into RNA, with the coding strand providing the template for RNA synthesis.
A transcription unit consists of a promoter, structural gene, and terminator which together regulate the transcription of a gene.
Monocistronic genes are those that code for a single polypeptide in eukaryotes, whereas polycistronic genes code for multiple polypeptides in bacteria.
Introns are non-coding regions of DNA that are spliced out of the primary transcript, while exons are coding regions that appear in mature RNA.

concepts.concept_title: Types of RNA and their Functions
concepts.description: mRNA provides the template, tRNA brings amino acids, and rRNA plays a structural and catalytic role during translation.

concepts.concept_title: RNA Polymerase and Initiation of Transcription
concepts.description: RNA polymerase binds to a promoter and initiates transcription, using nucleoside triphosphates as substrates.

concepts.concept_title: Template Dependent Polymerization by RNA Polymerase
concepts.description: RNA polymerase polymerizes RNA in a template dependent fashion following the rule of complementarity.
RNA polymerase, with the help of initiation-factor (σ), associates with the DNA template to initiate transcription.
RNA polymerase catalyzes the elongation of transcription, reading the template in a 3' - 5' direction.
RNA polymerase, with the help of termination-factor (ρ), associates with the Terminator region to terminate transcription.
Transcription and translation in bacteria often overlap because of the presence of intracellular ribosomes.

concepts.concept_title: Eukaryotic Transcription Initiated by Different RNA Polymerases
concepts.description: There are three primary types of RNA polymerases (I, II, and III) in eukaryotes, each transcribing specific types of RNA.
Introns are removed and exons are joined in a defined order in the process of splicing.
Capping and tailing processes modify hnRNA to form mRNA, which is then transported out of the nucleus for translation.
The process of copying one nucleic acid to form another nucleic acid, based on complementarity.
The process of transferring genetic information from a polymer of nucleotides to a polymer of amino acids.
A code that directs the sequence of amino acids during synthesis of proteins.
A code consisting of a combination of three nucleotides (codon) that codes for amino acids.
The process of determining the sequence of amino acids coded by a given sequence of nucleotides.
A code where some amino acids are coded by more than one codon.
Codons that do not code for any amino acids and function as terminating signals for translation.
A code that is nearly universal, with some exceptions found in mitochondria and protozoans.
A codon that codes for Methionine and acts as the initiator codon.
The process of predicting the sequence of amino acids coded by a given sequence of nucleotides.

concepts.concept_title: Sequence of Amino Acids to Nucleotide Sequence
concepts.description: The relationship between the sequence of amino acids coded by mRNA and the corresponding nucleotide sequence in RNA.
The effects of point mutations, specifically a change of single base pair, on genetic code and its consequences.
Insertion or deletion of one or two bases changes the reading frame from the point of insertion or deletion.
The effects of insertion or deletion of three or its multiple bases on amino acid synthesis and the reading frame.

concepts.concept_title: Role of tRNA as an Adapter Molecule
concepts.description: The function of tRNA as a molecule that reads the genetic code and links it to specific amino acids.
The composition and features of tRNA, including the anticodon loop and amino acid acceptor end.
A specific tRNA molecule that initiates translation, responsible for starting the process of protein synthesis.
tRNA has a compact molecular structure resembling an inverted L shape, which enables it to interact with ribosomes and transfer amino acids during translation.
A peptide bond is formed between two amino acids in the presence of energy, requiring the activation of amino acids through ATP and linking to their cognate tRNA.
Gene expression is regulated by metabolic, physiological, or environmental conditions, which may involve transcriptional initiation and the interaction of regulatory proteins with DNA.
Control of gene expression in prokaryotes is primarily achieved through the regulation of transcription initiation, influenced by accessory proteins and operators in DNA.
The lac operon is a model system for the study of transcriptional regulation, involving a polycistronic structural gene and a regulatory system consisting of operators and repressors.
An operon is a genetic arrangement found in bacteria, consisting of a regulatory gene and one or more structural genes.
The lac operon includes a regulatory gene (i gene) and three structural genes (z, y, and a), which code for the repressor, beta-galactosidase, permease, and transacetylase, respectively.

concepts.concept_title: Role of inducer and repressor proteins in operon regulation
concepts.description: Lactose acts as an inducer and inactivates the repressor protein, allowing RNA polymerase to transcribe the operon.
The regulation of the lac operon by the repressor is an example of negative regulation, where the repressor binds to the operator region and inhibits transcription.

concepts.concept_title: Regulation of enzyme synthesis by its substrate
concepts.description: The lac operon regulates enzyme synthesis by its substrate, lactose, where lactose acts as an inducer and activates the operon.
The project aimed to sequence human genome with the estimated 3 x 10^9 bp. The human genome is said to have approximately 3164.7 million base pairs.
The goals of HGP include identifying genes, determining DNA sequences, storing information in databases, and addressing ELSI.
Human genome size, average gene size, and estimated number of genes.

concepts.concept_title: Restriction Endonuclease Recognition Sites and Microsatellites
concepts.description: The use of restriction endonuclease recognition sites and repetitive DNA sequences (microsatellites) in genetic and physical maps.
The existence of variations in DNA sequences among individuals, such as restriction endonuclease recognition sites and microsatellites.
